tier_1_regimens.setting: Adjuvant treatment after surgery/biopsy when upfront RT and chemotherapy is not preferred, WHO grade 2, KPS ≥60; No residual/measurable disease after surgery/biopsy

trial_data.pfs: Median PFS 27.7 months vs. 11.1 months for placebo

tier_1_regimens.notes: Vorasidenib is a dual inhibitor of IDH1 and IDH2. In the phase 3 INDIGO study (Mellinghoff IK, et al. N Engl J Med 2023;389:589-601), vorasidenib improved median PFS (27.7 months vs. 11.1 months) compared to placebo in patients with residual or recurrent grade 2 IDH1/2-mutant glioma after surgery and no prior treatment. Overall survival data from this study are not yet available. Newly diagnosed patients with oligodendroglioma/astrocytoma (WHO grade 2 or 3) who did not have residual disease were excluded from the INDIGO study; thus it remains unknown if this subset would benefit from immediate treatment with an IDH inhibitor. The Panel strongly recommends multidisciplinary discussion for patients with newly diagnosed gliomas or if treatment with an IDH inhibitor is being considered.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Ivosidenib monotherapy (for IDH1 mutation, alternative to vorasidenib)
tier_1_regimens.setting: Adjuvant treatment after surgery/biopsy when upfront RT and chemotherapy is not preferred, WHO grade 2, KPS ≥60; No residual/measurable disease after surgery/biopsy
tier_1_regimens.notes: Ivosidenib is an IDH1 inhibitor and is a reasonable alternative if a patient cannot tolerate vorasidenib (Mellinghoff IK, et al. J Clin Oncol 2020;38:3398-3406). This option applies to IDH1-mutant tumors only.

tier_1_regimens.setting: Adjuvant treatment after surgery/biopsy, if initial treatment with RT and chemotherapy is preferred or after progression on IDH inhibitor, WHO grade 2, KPS ≥60

drugs.name: Vincristine
drugs.dose: PCV regimen doses as per protocol

trial_data.os: Significant improvement in median OS (median OS increased from 7.8 years to 13.3 years, P = .02); 10-year survival rate increased from 41% to 62%

tier_1_regimens.notes: Based on RTOG 9802 results, RT followed by 6 cycles of PCV is a category 1 recommendation. When PCV is recommended, carmustine may be substituted for lomustine. The Panel recommends that PCV be administered after RT (as per EORTC 26951) since the intensive PCV regimen given prior to RT (RTOG 9402) was not tolerated as well. A retrospective subgroup analysis suggests that the survival benefit with the addition of PCV was seen only in IDH-mutant tumors; patients with oligodendrogliomas benefited more than those with astrocytomas.

tier_1_regimens.setting: Adjuvant treatment after surgery/biopsy, if initial treatment with RT and chemotherapy is preferred or after progression on IDH inhibitor, WHO grade 2, KPS ≥60
tier_1_regimens.notes: RT followed by adjuvant temozolomide is an alternative to RT plus PCV. There is currently a lack of prospective randomized phase 3 data for the use of radiation and TMZ in patients with low-grade glioma, but data from EORTC and NCIC studies in glioblastoma support this regimen. PCV is generally more difficult to tolerate than TMZ, so it may be reasonable to treat elderly or comorbid patients with RT and TMZ instead.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: RT with concurrent and adjuvant temozolomide
tier_1_regimens.setting: Adjuvant treatment after surgery/biopsy, if initial treatment with RT and chemotherapy is preferred or after progression on IDH inhibitor, WHO grade 2, KPS ≥60

drugs.name: Temozolomide
drugs.schedule: Concurrent with RT and adjuvant following RT

tier_1_regimens.notes: Data from EORTC and NCIC studies, which included patients with glioblastoma, support RT with concurrent and adjuvant TMZ as an evidence-based regimen. This is an alternative to RT plus PCV.

trial_data.os: OS not reached in the RT + PCV group vs. 112 months in the RT group

tier_1_regimens.notes: The addition of PCV to RT for the treatment of newly diagnosed WHO grade 3 oligodendrogliomas is supported by results from two phase III trials: EORTC 26951 and RTOG 9402. The Panel recommends that PCV be administered after RT (as per EORTC 26951) since the intensive PCV regimen given prior to RT (RTOG 9402) was not tolerated as well and resulted in two deaths from PCV-induced neutropenia. In EORTC 26951, 70% of patients in the RT followed by PCV arm did not complete the planned six cycles of treatment.

tier_1_regimens.setting: Adjuvant treatment, KPS ≥60, age ≤70 years

drugs.name: Temozolomide
drugs.dose: 75 mg/m2 daily concurrent with RT, then 150-200 mg/m2 post-irradiation on 5-day schedule every 28 days
drugs.schedule: Concurrent with RT and adjuvant following RT

biomarkers.requirement: Not required but prognostic/predictive; methylated or unmethylated

tier_1_regimens.notes: In the EF-14 trial, patients with newly diagnosed glioblastoma and good PS (KPS ≥70) were randomized to TMZ alone or TMZ plus alternating electric field therapy following standard focal brain radiation and daily TMZ. The combination improved both median PFS and OS. Alternating electric field therapy is only an option for patients with supratentorial disease. Treatment with carmustine wafer may impact enrollment in adjuvant clinical trials.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: RT with concurrent and adjuvant temozolomide
tier_1_regimens.setting: Adjuvant treatment, KPS ≥60, age ≤70 years

drugs.name: Temozolomide
drugs.dose: 75 mg/m2 daily concurrent with RT, then 150-200 mg/m2 post-irradiation on 5-day schedule every 28 days
drugs.schedule: Concurrent with RT and adjuvant following RT

trial_data.os: Median OS 14.6 vs. 12.1 months; 5-year OS 10% vs. 2%

tier_1_regimens.notes: Adjuvant involved-field RT with concurrent and adjuvant TMZ is the standard recommended treatment based on the EORTC-NCIC trial. The TMZ dose used is 75 mg/m2 daily concurrent with RT, then 150 to 200 mg/m2 on a 5-day schedule every 28 days. There are no clear data that treatment with temozolomide beyond 6 cycles is beneficial, even in patients with MGMT promoter-methylated disease.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Hypofractionated RT plus concurrent and adjuvant temozolomide
tier_1_regimens.setting: Adjuvant treatment, KPS ≥60, age >70 years, MGMT promoter-methylated or indeterminate

drugs.name: Temozolomide

drugs.schedule: Concurrent with hypofractionated RT and adjuvant following RT

biomarkers.requirement: Preferred for methylated or indeterminate status

tier_1_regimens.notes: For patients >70 years with MGMT promoter-methylated or indeterminate tumors, hypofractionated brain RT plus concurrent and adjuvant TMZ is a category 1 recommendation. The greatest improvement in median OS was seen in patients with MGMT promoter-methylated tumors (13.5 months RT + TMZ vs. 7.7 months RT alone; HR, 0.53; 95% CI, 0.38-0.73; P < .001).

tier_1_regimens.setting: Poor PS (KPS <60)
tier_1_regimens.notes: Palliative/best supportive care is an option for patients with newly diagnosed glioblastoma with poor PS.

tier_1_regimens.site: Glioblastoma (IDH wild-type), Recurrent or Progressive Disease
tier_1_regimens.setting: Recurrent or progressive disease, WHO grade 3, KPS ≥60
tier_1_regimens.notes: Bevacizumab is FDA approved for the treatment of recurrent glioblastoma based on improvement in PFS. Although no studies of bevacizumab in patients with recurrent glioblastoma have demonstrated an improvement in survival, bevacizumab may suppress vascular permeability and provide radiographic appearance of response. Patients who have evidence of radiographic progression may benefit from continuation of bevacizumab to prevent rapid neurologic deterioration.

tier_1_regimens.notes: If there has been a long-time interval between stopping TMZ and development of tumor progression, it is reasonable to restart a patient on TMZ, particularly if the patient's tumor is MGMT methylated.

tier_1_regimens.setting: Recurrent or progressive disease, WHO grade 3, KPS ≥60
tier_1_regimens.notes: Lomustine is a reasonable second-line therapy, especially in a patient whose tumor is MGMT methylated.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Carmustine is a reasonable second-line therapy for recurrent glioblastoma.

tier_1_regimens.setting: Brain metastases from NTRK1/2/3 gene fusion-positive solid tumors
tier_1_regimens.notes: Larotrectinib is an NTRK inhibitor active in patients with NTRK gene fusion-positive solid tumors with brain metastases (Hong DS, et al. Lancet Oncol 2020;21:531-540).

tier_1_regimens.setting: Brain metastases from NTRK1/2/3 gene fusion-positive solid tumors
tier_1_regimens.notes: Entrectinib is an NTRK inhibitor active in patients with NTRK gene fusion-positive solid tumors with brain metastases (Doebele RC, et al. Lancet Oncol 2020;21:271-282).

tier_1_regimens.setting: Brain metastases from NTRK1/2/3 gene fusion-positive solid tumors
tier_1_regimens.notes: Repotrectinib is an NTRK inhibitor for NTRK gene fusion-positive solid tumors.

tier_1_regimens.setting: Brain metastases from BRAF non-specific melanoma

biomarkers.requirement: Not required for selection; preferred for BRAF non-specific melanoma

tier_1_regimens.notes: Two phase II trials support the use of combination ipilimumab and nivolumab for patients with asymptomatic untreated brain metastases from melanoma. In CheckMate 204, the intracranial response was 57.4% with complete response of 33%. The median duration of intracranial response was not reached at time of publication. Grade 3 or 4 treatment-related adverse events occurred in just over half of the patients.

tier_1_regimens.setting: Brain metastases from BRAF V600E-positive melanoma
tier_1_regimens.notes: The nonrandomized phase II COMBI-MB trial demonstrated clinical benefit and acceptable toxicity for dabrafenib/trametinib in patients with BRAF V600-mutant melanoma brain metastases. Among patients with asymptomatic brain metastases, an intracranial response was observed in 58% of those with untreated metastases.

tier_1_regimens.setting: Brain metastases from BRAF V600E-positive melanoma
tier_1_regimens.notes: Although there are no published prospective studies on the combination of vemurafenib and cobimetinib for melanoma patients with brain metastases, there is high-quality evidence that for melanoma with distant metastasis, combination therapy with vemurafenib and cobimetinib is associated with improved outcomes and safety compared with vemurafenib monotherapy.

tier_1_regimens.setting: Brain metastases from EGFR-sensitizing mutation-positive NSCLC
tier_1_regimens.notes: In the randomized phase III FLAURA trial, osimertinib was compared to a different EGFR-TKI (gefitinib or erlotinib) in 556 patients with previously untreated EGFR-sensitizing mutation-positive NSCLC. Patients with CNS metastases had median PFS of 15.2 months vs. 9.6 months (HR, 0.47; P < .001). CNS ORR was 91% vs. 68% (P = .066).

tier_1_regimens.setting: Brain metastases from ALK gene fusion-positive NSCLC
tier_1_regimens.notes: In a randomized phase III trial comparing brigatinib to crizotinib, intracranial response was more likely in the brigatinib arm than in the crizotinib arm (67% vs. 17%). Twelve-month survival without intracranial disease progression was 67% for brigatinib vs. 21% for crizotinib (HR, 0.27; 95% CI, 0.13-0.54).

tier_1_regimens.setting: Brain metastases from ALK gene fusion-positive NSCLC
tier_1_regimens.notes: Post hoc analysis of the CROWN trial showed that in patients with brain metastases at baseline, complete CNS response was observed in 61% of patients who received lorlatinib, compared to 15% in those who received crizotinib.

tier_1_regimens.setting: Brain metastases from ALK gene fusion-positive NSCLC
tier_1_regimens.notes: In a randomized phase III trial comparing alectinib to crizotinib, CNS response was observed in 81% of patients in the alectinib arm (8 complete responses) and 50% in the crizotinib arm (1 complete response).

tier_1_regimens.setting: Brain metastases from HER2 (ERBB2) positive breast cancer, previously treated with ≥1 regimen
tier_1_regimens.notes: In the HER2CLIMB phase III trial, tucatinib combined with trastuzumab and capecitabine was compared to placebo plus trastuzumab and capecitabine in patients with HER2-positive metastatic breast cancer previously treated with HER2-directed therapy. Among patients with brain metastases at baseline, both PFS and OS were superior in the tucatinib arm.

tier_1_regimens.setting: Brain metastases from HER2 (ERBB2) positive breast cancer, previously treated with ≥1 regimen
tier_1_regimens.notes: Subgroup analysis from the DESTINY-Breast01 trial showed activity of fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki in patients with HER2-positive breast cancer with brain metastases.

tier_1_regimens.site: Small Cell Lung Cancer with Brain Metastases
tier_1_regimens.setting: Brain metastases from SCLC
tier_1_regimens.notes: Tarlatamab-dlle is a bispecific T-cell engager for the treatment of SCLC.

tier_1_regimens.site: Renal Cell Carcinoma with Brain Metastases
tier_1_regimens.setting: Brain metastases from renal cell carcinoma
tier_1_regimens.notes: Cabozantinib has demonstrated activity in patients with RCC and brain metastases.

tier_1_regimens.setting: Induction therapy, newly diagnosed
tier_1_regimens.notes: High doses of intravenous methotrexate (≥3.5 g/m2) are necessary to overcome the BBB and achieve therapeutic levels in the CSF. Consider glucarpidase for prolonged methotrexate clearance due to methotrexate-induced renal toxicity.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: High-Dose Methotrexate 8 g/m2 combined with Rituximab
tier_1_regimens.setting: Induction therapy, newly diagnosed
tier_1_regimens.notes: High-dose methotrexate combined with rituximab is a preferred induction regimen. Hepatitis B testing is indicated because of the risk of reactivation with immunotherapy plus systemic therapy.

tier_1_regimens.setting: Induction therapy, newly diagnosed; consider WBRT
tier_1_regimens.notes: MPV + Rituximab is a preferred induction regimen for PCNSL.

tier_1_regimens.setting: Induction therapy, newly diagnosed
tier_1_regimens.notes: MT + Rituximab is a preferred induction regimen for PCNSL.

tier_1_regimens.setting: Induction therapy, newly diagnosed
tier_1_regimens.notes: MATRIX is associated with significant myeloid toxicity. This regimen is based on the IELSG32 trial results (Ferreri AJM, et al. Lancet Haematol 2016;3:e217-227).

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Cytarabine/Thiotepa followed by Carmustine/Thiotepa (high-dose systemic therapy with stem cell rescue)
tier_1_regimens.setting: Consolidation therapy after CR or CRu
tier_1_regimens.notes: High-dose systemic therapy with stem cell rescue is a preferred consolidation option after CR or CRu. The risk of neurotoxicity should be considered before administering high-dose therapy to a patient with prior WBRT.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: TBC (Thiotepa/Busulfan/Cyclophosphamide/Mesna) (high-dose systemic therapy with stem cell rescue)
tier_1_regimens.setting: Consolidation therapy after CR or CRu
tier_1_regimens.notes: TBC is a preferred high-dose consolidation regimen with stem cell rescue.

tier_1_regimens.setting: Consolidation therapy after CR or CRu
tier_1_regimens.notes: EA consolidation was evaluated in the Alliance 50202 trial.

tier_1_regimens.setting: Consolidation therapy after CR or CRu
tier_1_regimens.notes: High-dose cytarabine is a preferred consolidation option.

tier_1_regimens.setting: Relapsed or refractory disease
tier_1_regimens.notes: Ibrutinib is associated with risk of Aspergillus infection. This is a recommended regimen for relapsed/refractory PCNSL.

tier_1_regimens.setting: Relapsed or refractory disease
tier_1_regimens.notes: MBVP + Rituximab is a recommended regimen for relapsed/refractory PCNSL.

tier_1_regimens.setting: Relapsed or refractory disease
tier_1_regimens.notes: Lenalidomide + Rituximab is a recommended regimen for relapsed/refractory PCNSL.

tier_1_regimens.setting: Relapsed or refractory disease
tier_1_regimens.notes: Tisagenlecleucel was added to the guidelines for relapsed/refractory PCNSL based on a phase 1/2 clinical trial (Frigault MJ, et al. Blood 2022;139:2306-2315).

tier_1_regimens.setting: Relapsed or refractory disease
tier_1_regimens.notes: Axicabtagene ciloleucel was added based on a pilot study in relapsed/refractory primary and secondary CNS lymphoma (Nayak L, et al. ASCO Annual Meeting 2024).

tier_1_regimens.setting: Relapsed or refractory disease
tier_1_regimens.notes: Ibrutinib/Lenalidomide + Rituximab was added based on a phase Ib study with expansion (Schaff LR, et al. Neuro Oncol 2025;27:2107-2116).

tier_1_regimens.setting: Recurrent/progressive meningioma
tier_1_regimens.notes: A prospective, multicenter, nonrandomized, phase II trial evaluating sunitinib in 36 heavily pretreated patients with refractory meningioma showed a 6-month PFS rate of 42%, with a median PFS rate of 5.2 months and a median OS rate of 24.6 months (Kaley TJ, et al. Neuro Oncol 2015;17:116-121). Toxicities were considerable, with 60% of patients experiencing grade 3 or higher toxicity.

tier_1_regimens.setting: Recurrent/progressive meningioma
tier_1_regimens.notes: Abemaciclib was added to the guidelines based on a multicenter basket trial for CNS tumors that identified activity of the CDK4/6 inhibitor abemaciclib in recurrent meningioma (Kaley TJ, et al. Neuro Oncol 2025;27:3189-3199).

tier_1_regimens.setting: Recurrent/progressive meningioma
tier_1_regimens.notes: Retrospective data support the use of bevacizumab for patients with recurrent meningioma, especially for patients with symptoms driven by RT necrosis, with a 6-month PFS rate of 43.8% for recurrent surgery and radiation-refractory grade 2 and 3 meningioma.

tier_1_regimens.setting: Recurrent/progressive meningioma
tier_1_regimens.notes: Octreotide acetate is a somatostatin analogue useful in certain circumstances for recurrent meningioma.

tier_1_regimens.setting: Recurrent/progressive meningioma
tier_1_regimens.notes: In a phase II trial evaluating bevacizumab combined with everolimus for recurrent meningioma (N = 17), stable disease was reported in 88% of patients, with median PFS and OS of 22.0 months and 23.8 months, respectively.

tier_1_regimens.setting: Leptomeningeal metastases from EGFR mutation-positive NSCLC (for exon 19 deletion or L858R)
tier_1_regimens.notes: Amivantamab and hyaluronidase-lpuj subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV amivantamab-vmjw. This combination has different dosing and administration instructions compared to IV amivantamab-vmjw.

tier_1_regimens.setting: Leptomeningeal metastases from HER2-positive breast cancer
tier_1_regimens.notes: Fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki is the preferred systemic therapy option for leptomeningeal metastases from HER2-positive breast cancer.

tier_1_regimens.setting: Leptomeningeal metastases from melanoma
tier_1_regimens.notes: Intrathecal and intravenous nivolumab was evaluated in a phase 1 trial for melanoma with leptomeningeal disease (Glitza Oliva IC, et al. Nat Med 2023;29:898-905).

tier_1_regimens.setting: Progressive or symptomatic vestibular schwannoma
tier_1_regimens.notes: Treatment with bevacizumab results in hearing improvement in 36% of patients with NF2-SWN with hearing loss due to VS, stabilization in 46%, and deterioration in 18%. Treatment results in a partial VS volume reduction (≥20%) on MRI in 40% of patients and disease stabilization in 50%, while 10% experienced tumor progression.

tier_1_regimens.setting: Progressive or symptomatic vestibular schwannoma
tier_1_regimens.notes: Brigatinib was evaluated in a study of NF2-related schwannomatosis with progressive tumors (Plotkin SR, et al. N Engl J Med 2024;390:2284-2294).

tier_1_regimens.setting: VHL-associated CNS hemangioblastomas not requiring immediate surgery
tier_1_regimens.notes: Belzutifan has been FDA-approved for the treatment of VHL-associated CNS hemangioblastomas not requiring immediate surgery. Based on a nonrandomized phase 2 trial including patients with VHL-associated renal cell carcinoma (N = 61), objective response in patients with CNS hemangioblastoma was 30% (Jonasch E, et al. N Engl J Med 2021;385:2036-2046).

tier_1_regimens.site: Primary Spinal Cord Tumors / NF1-associated plexiform neurofibromas
tier_1_regimens.setting: Symptomatic plexiform neurofibromas not amenable to complete resection
tier_1_regimens.notes: Selumetinib is a category 1 recommendation for patients with NF1 who have symptomatic plexiform neurofibromas not amenable to complete resection.

tier_1_regimens.site: Primary Spinal Cord Tumors / NF1-associated plexiform neurofibromas
tier_1_regimens.setting: Symptomatic plexiform neurofibromas not amenable to complete resection
tier_1_regimens.notes: Mirdametinib was evaluated in a pivotal phase IIb trial (ReNeu) in adults and children with symptomatic NF1-associated plexiform neurofibromas (Moertel CL, et al. J Clin Oncol 2025;43:716-729).

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Dabrafenib/Trametinib for circumscribed glioma with BRAF alterations
tier_1_regimens.setting: Adjuvant treatment for PA, circumscribed ganglioglioma/glioneuronal tumor, PXA (grade 2) with BRAF V600E activation mutation; or recurrent disease with BRAF alterations
tier_1_regimens.notes: The phase II VE-BASKET study showed that vemurafenib was efficacious in BRAF-mutated low-grade gliomas, particularly PXA. A phase II trial including 13 patients with BRAF-mutated low-grade glioma showed that dabrafenib/trametinib was associated with an ORR of 69%.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Cobimetinib/Vemurafenib for circumscribed glioma with BRAF alterations
tier_1_regimens.setting: Adjuvant treatment for PA, circumscribed ganglioglioma/glioneuronal tumor, PXA (grade 2) with BRAF V600E activation mutation; or recurrent disease with BRAF alterations
tier_1_regimens.notes: Cobimetinib/Vemurafenib is useful in certain circumstances for BRAF V600E-mutant circumscribed gliomas.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Larotrectinib for circumscribed glioma with NTRK1/2/3 gene fusions
tier_1_regimens.setting: Recurrent or progressive disease with NTRK1/2/3 gene fusions
tier_1_regimens.notes: Larotrectinib is an NTRK inhibitor for tumors with NTRK gene fusions.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Entrectinib for circumscribed glioma with NTRK1/2/3 gene fusions
tier_1_regimens.setting: Recurrent or progressive disease with NTRK1/2/3 gene fusions
tier_1_regimens.notes: Entrectinib is an NTRK inhibitor for tumors with NTRK gene fusions.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Repotrectinib for circumscribed glioma with NTRK1/2/3 gene fusions
tier_1_regimens.setting: Recurrent or progressive disease with NTRK1/2/3 gene fusions
tier_1_regimens.notes: Repotrectinib is an NTRK inhibitor for tumors with NTRK gene fusions.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Tovorafenib for BRAF gene fusion or BRAF V600 mutation
tier_1_regimens.setting: Recurrent or progressive disease with BRAF gene fusion or BRAF V600 mutation

biomarkers.marker: BRAF gene fusion or BRAF V600 mutation

tier_1_regimens.notes: Tovorafenib is a type II RAF inhibitor evaluated in relapsed/refractory pediatric low-grade glioma in the phase 2 FIREFLY-1 trial (Kilburn LB, et al. Nat Med 2024;30:207-217).

tier_1_regimens.setting: Recurrent disease
tier_1_regimens.notes: Temozolomide was evaluated in a retrospective study for salvage treatment of recurrent intracranial ependymomas in adults (Ruda R, et al. Neuro Oncol 2016;18:261-268).

tier_1_regimens.setting: Recurrent disease
tier_1_regimens.notes: Platinum-based regimens including carboplatin/etoposide have been used for recurrent ependymoma.

tier_1_regimens.setting: Recurrent disease
tier_1_regimens.notes: Bevacizumab was evaluated for recurrent ependymoma (Green RM, et al. Neurology 2009;73:1677-1680).

tier_1_regimens.setting: Recurrent disease
tier_1_regimens.notes: The combination of lapatinib and dose-dense temozolomide was evaluated in a phase II trial in patients with recurrent grade 1, 2, and 3 ependymoma (Gilbert MR, et al. Neuro Oncol 2021;23:468-477).

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Cisplatin/Cyclophosphamide/Vincristine (following weekly vincristine with concurrent radiation during craniospinal RT)
tier_1_regimens.setting: Adjuvant treatment following craniospinal RT for standard-risk and high-risk disease
tier_1_regimens.notes: Omission of vincristine during the radiotherapy phase of therapy or dose modification may be required for adults because they do not tolerate this regimen as well. Data supporting vincristine's use have been found in pediatric trials only.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Cisplatin/Lomustine/Vincristine (following weekly vincristine with concurrent radiation during craniospinal RT)
tier_1_regimens.setting: Adjuvant treatment following craniospinal RT for standard-risk and high-risk disease
tier_1_regimens.notes: Omission of vincristine during the radiotherapy phase of therapy or dose modification may be required for adults because they do not tolerate this regimen as well.

tier_1_regimens.setting: Recurrent disease, no prior systemic therapy or prior systemic therapy
tier_1_regimens.notes: High-dose Cyclophosphamide/Etoposide is recommended for recurrent medulloblastoma in patients with no prior or prior systemic therapy.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Vismodegib (for SHH pathway-mutant medulloblastoma with prior systemic therapy)
tier_1_regimens.setting: Recurrent disease with SHH pathway mutation and prior systemic therapy
tier_1_regimens.notes: Patients in trials with SHH-activated disease were more likely to respond than patients with non-SHH disease (Robinson GW, et al. J Clin Oncol 2015;33:2646-2654).

tier_1_regimens.setting: Recurrent H3K27M-mutant diffuse glioma
tier_1_regimens.notes: Dordaviprone is a caseinolytic protease P (ClpP) agonist and dopamine receptor D2 (DRD2) antagonist. In adult and pediatric patients with recurrent H3 K27M-mutant diffuse glioma who were evaluated by Blinded Independent Central Review (BICR)-confirmed RANO 2.0 criteria, dordaviprone monotherapy demonstrated a 28.0% overall response rate (ORR), 4.6-month median time to recovery (mTTR), 10.4-month median duration of response (mDOR), and 13.7-month median overall survival (Arrillaga-Romany I, et al. J Clin Oncol 2024;42:1542-1552).

tier_2_handbook.definition: Central nervous system (CNS) cancers encompass a heterogeneous group of neoplasms arising within the brain, spinal cord, meninges, and related structures. The NCCN Guidelines for CNS Cancers (Version 2.2026) focus on management of adult CNS cancers including glioma (WHO grade 1, oligodendroglioma [IDH-mutant, 1p19q codeleted], IDH-mutant astrocytoma, glioblastoma), intracranial and spinal ependymomas, medulloblastoma, primary CNS lymphomas, meningiomas, primary spinal cord tumors, brain metastases, leptomeningeal metastases, and metastatic spine tumors [1,2]. The WHO 2021 Classification of Tumors of the Central Nervous System reclassified adult diffuse gliomas into three principal molecular subtypes: IDH-mutant astrocytoma, oligodendroglioma (IDH-mutant, 1p19q codeleted), and glioblastoma (IDH-wildtype) [6]. Primary brain tumors are derived from glial cells (astrocytes, oligodendrocytes, ependymal cells), neuronal or glioneuronal elements, or meningeal arachnoid cap cells. Brain metastases are the most common intracranial tumors in adults, occurring up to 10 times more frequently than primary brain tumors [443-445]. CNS tumors are associated with symptoms including seizures, fatigue, psychiatric disorders, impaired mobility, neurocognitive dysfunction, difficulty speaking, short-term memory problems, and complications such as intracerebral edema, endocrinopathies, and venous thromboembolism that can seriously impact quality of life [2].

subtypes.name: Circumscribed Glioma (WHO Grade 1)
subtypes.description: Non-invasive, generally surgically curable low-grade gliomas including pilocytic astrocytoma (PA), subependymal giant cell astrocytoma (SEGA), ganglioglioma, dysembryoplastic neuroepithelial tumor (DNET), and pleomorphic xanthoastrocytoma (PXA). These tumors range from the very uncommon, noninvasive, and surgically curable pilocytic astrocytomas to glioblastoma, the most common malignant brain tumor in adults [2].
subtypes.frequency: Low-grade gliomas comprise approximately 5% to 10% of all CNS tumors [74]. Most WHO grade 1 gliomas are curable by surgery alone.
key_associations: BRAF V600E mutation in 60%–80% of supratentorial PXAs, 30% of DNETs, 20% of grade 1 gangliogliomas, and 5% of PAs [MS-6]
BRAF fusions (KIAA1549-BRAF) in most pediatric PAs, especially posterior fossa tumors
Tuberous sclerosis complex (TSC) association for SEGAs (occurring in 5%–20% of TSC individuals) [85-87]
NF2 association for certain meningiomas and schwannomas

subtypes.name: Oligodendroglioma, WHO Grade 2 (IDH1/2-mutant, 1p19q codeleted)
subtypes.description: Diffusely infiltrative gliomas defined by the co-occurrence of IDH1/2 mutation and whole-arm 1p/19q codeletion. Radiographically, these tumors appear well demarcated, occasionally contain calcifications, and do not often enhance with contrast. In histology, the typical 'fried egg' appearance is evident as a fixation artifact in paraffin [MS-9]. Survival rates tend to be better in oligodendrogliomas than in other gliomas.
subtypes.frequency: Among WHO grade 2–3 gliomas, oligodendrogliomas represent one of three major molecular subgroups [9,13]. They are less common than diffuse astrocytomas.
key_associations: IDH1/2 mutation (defining feature)
Whole-arm 1p/19q codeletion [translocation t(1;19)(q10;p10)] (defining feature) [22]
TERT promoter mutations (nearly always present) [28,29]
MGMT promoter methylation (commonly associated) [10]
CTNNB1 mutations in WNT-activated subtype

subtypes.name: Oligodendroglioma, WHO Grade 3 (IDH1/2-mutant, 1p19q codeleted)
subtypes.description: Higher-grade counterpart of grade 2 oligodendrogliomas with the same defining molecular features (IDH mutation and 1p/19q codeletion) but with more aggressive histopathology. Conventionally treated with similar radiation doses as WHO grade 3 and 4 astrocytomas but generally at lower dose per fraction (1.8 Gy/fraction vs. 2.0 Gy/fraction) to decrease risk of late side effects [MS-13].
subtypes.frequency: Relatively rare; among high-grade gliomas, WHO grade 3 oligodendrogliomas have a much better prognosis compared to grade 3 IDH-mutant astrocytomas and glioblastomas [MS-12].
key_associations: Same molecular hallmarks as grade 2: IDH1/2 mutation, 1p/19q codeletion, TERT promoter mutation
Significant improvement in median OS with addition of PCV (procarbazine, lomustine, vincristine) to RT [EORTC 26951: HR, 0.56; 95% CI, 0.31–1.03 for 1p/19q codeleted subgroup], [161,162]
Significant OS benefit with PCV plus RT vs. RT alone in 1p/19q codeleted subgroup [RTOG 9402: median OS 14.7 vs. 7.3 years; HR, 0.59; 95% CI, 0.37–0.95; P = .03], [163,164]

subtypes.name: IDH-Mutant Astrocytoma, WHO Grade 2
subtypes.description: Diffusely infiltrative astrocytomas defined by IDH1/2 mutation WITHOUT 1p/19q codeletion, and WITHOUT the histopathologic features of glioblastoma (necrosis and/or microvascular proliferation). These tumors have a more favorable prognosis than IDH-wildtype glioblastomas but generally progress faster than grade 2 oligodendrogliomas [MS-9].
subtypes.frequency: More common than grade 2 oligodendrogliomas in some series; part of the IDH-mutant subgroup of grade 2–3 gliomas [9,13].
key_associations: IDH1/2 mutation (defining feature)
Loss of ATRX (nearly always mutually exclusive with 1p/19q codeletion) [27,38,39]
TP53 mutations (diagnostic of astrocytoma when combined with IDH mutation and ATRX loss) [27]
ABSENCE of TERT promoter mutation (suggests astrocytoma) [28,29]
Vorasidenib improved median PFS vs. placebo (27.7 months vs. 11.1 months) in grade 2 IDH1/2-mutant glioma [15]

subtypes.name: IDH-Mutant Astrocytoma, WHO Grade 3
subtypes.description: Higher-grade IDH-mutant astrocytomas without the molecular hallmarks of glioblastoma. Strict histopathologic criteria do not currently exist for definitively diagnosing a WHO grade 2 versus a WHO grade 3 IDH-mutated astrocytoma [GLIO-5 footnotes]. Some panel members feel that it may be reasonable to try treating patients with newly diagnosed IDH1/2-mutated grade 3 astrocytoma upfront with an IDH inhibitor in certain circumstances.
subtypes.frequency: Part of the spectrum of IDH-mutant astrocytomas; intermediate between grade 2 and grade 4.
key_associations: IDH1/2 mutation, ATRX loss, TP53 mutation (no 1p/19q codeletion)
Benefit from adjuvant TMZ shown in CATNON trial interim analysis [167]
CATNON second interim analysis: OS benefit with adjuvant TMZ in IDH-mutant subgroup (HR, 0.48; 95% CI, 0.35–0.67; P < .0001) but not in IDH-wildtype (HR, 1.00; 95% CI, 0.75–1.33; P = 0.98) [167]

subtypes.name: IDH-Mutant Astrocytoma, WHO Grade 4
subtypes.description: Diffusely infiltrative astrocytomas with IDH1/2 mutation that have either: (1) traditional grade 4 histologic features (necrosis and/or microvascular proliferation), OR (2) homozygous deletion in CDKN2A/B without necrosis/MVP. These are now distinguished from IDH-wildtype glioblastoma, as they have better clinical outcomes [6,23,30-33].
subtypes.frequency: A subset of IDH-mutant astrocytomas; relatively uncommon compared to glioblastoma.
key_associations: IDH1/2 mutation with either grade 4 histologic features or CDKN2A/B homozygous deletion
Better prognosis than IDH-wildtype glioblastoma
Treatment extrapolated from interim analyses of data from the CATNON study [GLIO-6 footnotes]
Standard RT + concurrent and adjuvant temozolomide ± alternating electric field therapy

subtypes.name: Glioblastoma (IDH-Wildtype)
subtypes.description: The most common malignant brain tumor in adults, characterized by IDH1/2 wild-type status. The term 'glioblastoma' is now reserved exclusively for IDH-wildtype gliomas. Some IDH-wildtype gliomas may lack histologic features of glioblastoma but have molecular hallmarks including: IDH1/2 wild-type; EGFR gene amplification; gain of chromosome 7 and loss of chromosome 10; and/or TERT promoter mutation [22,24]. Median survival remains poor despite aggressive multimodal therapy.
subtypes.frequency: Most common malignant primary brain tumor in adults; accounts for more than half of all malignant primary CNS tumors [2]. 5-year survival rates around 6%, with higher rates among younger age groups [74].
key_associations: IDH1/2 wild-type status (defining feature)
EGFR gene amplification
Gain of chromosome 7 and loss of chromosome 10
TERT promoter mutation
MGMT promoter methylation (predictive biomarker for response to temozolomide) [33,51]
CDKN2A/B homozygous deletion
Standard treatment: RT + concurrent temozolomide + adjuvant temozolomide ± tumor-treating fields (TTFields) [category 1 recommendation], [142,178,179]
Approximately 2% have DNA replication repair deficiency (TMB-H, dMMR) [BRAIN-E 5 of 10]

subtypes.name: High-Grade Glioma: Other
subtypes.description: Includes WHO grade 3 PXA, high-grade astrocytoma with piloid features (HGAP), and H3-mutated high-grade glioma. These are relatively rare subtypes that require molecular testing to guide treatment decisions. Treatment recommendations follow general high-grade glioma pathways with consideration of specific molecular markers.
subtypes.frequency: Rare subtypes; collectively constitute a small fraction of high-grade gliomas.
key_associations: PXA WHO grade 3: BRAF V600E mutation in 60%–80% [MS-6]
HGAP: BRAF fusions may be present
H3-mutated high-grade glioma: H3K27M mutation in H3-3A gene [34,35]; dordaviprone demonstrated 28.0% ORR, 4.6-month mTTR, 10.4-month mDOR, and 13.7-month median OS [s,70]

subtypes.name: Intracranial and Spinal Ependymoma
subtypes.description: Ependymal tumors arising from ependymal cells lining the ventricular system and spinal canal. In adults, ependymomas occur more often in the spinal canal than in the intracranial compartment. These tumors can cause hydrocephalus, increased intracranial pressure, mimic brainstem lesions, and cause cranial nerve palsies [216,217]. Posterior fossa ependymomas are categorized as two groups: A (PFA) and B (PFB), with PFA more aggressive [24,218,224].
subtypes.frequency: Ependymomas constitute up to 1.6% of CNS tumors [74]. CSF dissemination develops in up to 15% of intracranial ependymomas.
key_associations: ZFTA gene fusions (RELA activating fusions in ~19% of ependymomas) [220]
YAP1 gene fusions (supratentorial)
MYCN gene amplification in aggressive spinal ependymomas [222,223]
H3K27 trimethylation loss characteristic of PFA ependymomas [24,218,224]
Spinal myxopapillary ependymomas: improved disease control with postoperative RT [399,400]

subtypes.name: Sellar Tumors (Pituitary Adenomas and Craniopharyngiomas)
subtypes.description: Tumors arising in the sellar region including pituitary adenomas (functioning and non-functioning), craniopharyngiomas (papillary and adamantinomatous), pituitary carcinomas, and other sellar masses. Papillary craniopharyngiomas are driven by BRAF V600E mutations. Treatment approaches vary widely based on tumor type, hormone secretion status, and clinical presentation.
subtypes.frequency: Pituitary adenomas are common incidental findings. Craniopharyngiomas are rare epithelial tumors.
key_associations: BRAF V600E in papillary craniopharyngiomas [11,12]
BRAF-MEK inhibitors (cobimetinib/vemurafenib) for papillary craniopharyngiomas [category 1 evidence], [12]
Dopamine agonists for prolactinomas [1]
Somatostatin analogs for GH-secreting tumors
Temozolomide for pituitary carcinoma

subtypes.name: Adult Medulloblastoma
subtypes.description: WHO grade 4 embryonal tumors predominantly arising from the cerebellum. Although most common in children, they also occur in adults, comprising 1% of CNS tumors in adults [260]. Adult medulloblastoma is genetically distinct from pediatric medulloblastoma with differing prognostic markers. Tumors can be classified into WNT-activated, SHH-activated (TP53-mutant and TP53-wildtype), and non-WNT/non-SHH subgroups [6,259,262].
subtypes.frequency: 1% of CNS tumors in adults [260]; adult medulloblastoma is a rare adult CNS malignancy requiring specialized centers.
key_associations: WNT-activated subtype: CTNNB1 mutations; germline APC mutations less common [259,268]; markedly better prognosis regardless of age [259]
SHH-activated, TP53-mutant: poor prognosis [269]
SHH-activated, TP53-wildtype: variable course [269]
Non-WNT/non-SHH: variable course [259]
6q loss not prognostic in adult medulloblastoma (unlike pediatric) [265]
Metastatic disease less common in adults than children [267]

subtypes.name: Primary CNS Lymphoma (PCNSL)
subtypes.description: An aggressive form of non-Hodgkin lymphoma developing within the brain, spinal cord, eye, or leptomeninges without evidence of systemic involvement. Pathologically, PCNSL is an angiocentric neoplasm composed of dense monoclonal proliferation of lymphocytes, usually diffuse large B cells, with more than 90% being activated B-cell-like (ABC) subtype [306,307]. Brain parenchyma is involved in more than 90% of patients and disease is multifocal in more than 50% of cases.
subtypes.frequency: Accounts for approximately 3% of all neoplasms and 4%–6% of all extranodal lymphomas [301]. Overall incidence in immunocompetent patients is 0.47 per 100,000 person-years with higher incidence in males [301]. Greater incidence in older adults (1.8 per 100,000 in patients aged ≥65 years) [301,302].
key_associations: MYD88 mutations (detectable by PCR in CSF)
High-dose methotrexate-based regimens are standard induction therapy
Leptomeningeal involvement in up to 30% of patients
Ocular involvement in 10%–20% of patients (can develop independently)
HIV association (distinct management pathway)
Post-transplant lymphoproliferative disorders (PTLDs) in 7%–15% of transplant recipients [309-312]

subtypes.name: Primary Spinal Cord Tumors
subtypes.description: Histologically diverse set of tumors representing 2%–4% of all primary CNS tumors [388]. Extramedullary lesions (most commonly benign meningiomas) account for 70%–80% of spinal cord tumors [389]. Astrocytomas (more prevalent in children) and ependymomas (more prevalent in adults) are the most common intramedullary tumors. Since 70% are low-grade and slow-growing, patients often suffer pain for months to years before diagnosis [388].
subtypes.frequency: Overall incidence is 0.74 per 100,000 person-years with a 10-year survival rate of 64% [388].
key_associations: Type 1 NF association with spinal astrocytomas
Type 2 NF association with spinal peripheral nerve sheath tumors
VHL syndrome association with intramedullary hemangioblastomas
Hemangioblastoma: belzutifan FDA-approved for VHL-associated CNS hemangioblastomas not requiring immediate surgery [405]

subtypes.name: Meningiomas
subtypes.description: Extra-axial CNS tumors arising from arachnoid cap cells in the meninges. Most often discovered in middle-to-late adult life with a female predominance. Radiographic features include dural-based, homogeneously contrast-enhancing mass with dural tail and CSF cleft. Histologically graded as WHO grade 1 (benign, 92%), grade 2 (atypical, 6%), or grade 3 (malignant/anaplastic, 2%) [408,409].
subtypes.frequency: Annual incidence: 1.8 per 100,000 in males and 3.4 per 100,000 in females [408]. Seizure is a common presenting symptom in 27% of patients [411]. Small tumors are often asymptomatic incidental findings [410].
key_associations: NF2-SWN association (bilateral vestibular schwannomas, multiple meningiomas)
Overexpression of somatostatin receptors (SSTRs) allowing octreotide imaging [413-415]
Genomic copy number profiling: concomitant loss of 22q and 1p suggests higher grade [84]
Homozygous loss of CDKN2A/B or TERT promoter mutation justifies WHO grade 3 assignment [84]
DNA methylation profiling for prognostic stratification [84-86]

subtypes.name: Limited Brain Metastases
subtypes.description: Brain metastases for which SRS represents an effective alternative to WBRT, with more cognitive protection. The definition of 'limited' in terms of number of metastases or total intracranial disease volume is evolving and may depend on the specific clinical situation [Yamamoto M, et al. Lancet Oncol 2014;15:387-395 and Aizer AA, et al. JAMA 2026]. Primary lung cancers are the most common source [446], and melanoma has the highest rates of brain metastases among patients with metastatic disease [447,448].
subtypes.frequency: About 8%–10% of cancer patients are affected by symptomatic metastatic brain tumors [443,444]. On autopsy, brain metastases are present in 25% of patients with cancer [445].
key_associations: Nearly 80% occur in cerebral hemispheres, 15% in cerebellum, 5% in brainstem [450]
Hematogenous spread to gray-white junction
SRS is preferred: comparable OS and superior cognitive preservation vs. WBRT [463-466]
Tumor volume and rate of new CNS lesions may be more important prognostic factors than number of discrete metastases [468-471]

subtypes.name: Extensive Brain Metastases
subtypes.description: Brain metastases that do not fit the definition of 'limited brain metastases.' Management generally involves WBRT (with hippocampal avoidance and memantine if eligible) or SRS in selected patients with good performance status and low overall tumor volume. The optimal treatment strategy is highly individualized.
subtypes.frequency: Patients with diffuse or numerous brain metastases, high brain metastasis velocity, or leptomeningeal disease.
key_associations: HA-WBRT + memantine preferred for patients with ≥4 months life expectancy and no metastases within 5mm of hippocampi [495]
Standard WBRT dose: 30 Gy in 10 fractions [495]
Short-course WBRT (20 Gy in 5 fractions) for poor prognosis patients
SRS may be considered in select patients with good PS and low overall tumor volume

subtypes.name: Leptomeningeal Metastases
subtypes.description: Malignant cells' multifocal seeding of the leptomeninges. Known as leptomeningeal carcinomatosis when originating from solid tumors, lymphomatous meningitis for lymphoma, and leukemic meningitis for leukemia. Occurs in approximately 5% of cancer patients [579]. Most cases arise from breast cancers, lung cancers, and melanoma [580,581]. Median survival is typically 2–4 months [580,581].
subtypes.frequency: Approximately 5% of patients with cancer [579]. Being diagnosed with increasing frequency as patients live longer with improved systemic therapeutics.
key_associations: Brain parenchymal involvement in >90% of patients
Leptomeningeal involvement in up to 30% of PCNSL patients
CSF cytology has ~50% sensitivity on first lumbar puncture, up to 90% after repeated analyses [585,586]
Breast, lung, and melanoma have highest rates of leptomeningeal spread [580,581]

subtypes.name: Metastatic Spine Tumors
subtypes.description: Bone metastases involving the spinal column, the most frequently affected site of bony metastases. Spinal metastases primarily arise from breast, lung, prostate, and renal cancers [611,612]. Extradural lesions account for about 95% of spinal tumors, mostly in the thoracic region. Spinal cord compression affects 5%–10% of all cancer patients with more than 20,000 cases diagnosed annually in the United States [613].
subtypes.frequency: Spinal cord compression: 5%–10% of all cancer patients, >20,000 cases/year in US [613].
key_associations: 15%–20% of patients have additional spine lesions; highly recommend complete spine imaging [SPINE-1]
Surgery followed by RT preferred for spinal cord compression from solid tumors [category 1], [626]
Spinal instability evaluated using Spinal Instability Neoplastic Score [645]
SBRT preferred for oligometastatic disease and radioresistant histologies

subtypes.name: NF2-Related Schwannomatosis (NF2-SWN)
subtypes.description: An autosomal dominant tumor predisposition syndrome caused by mutations in the NF2 gene (merlin gene) on chromosome 22q12. Characterized by development of multiple CNS tumors including bilateral vestibular schwannomas (VS), multiple meningiomas, ependymomas, and schwannomas. The condition results in significant morbidity from hearing loss, neurologic deficits, and the challenges of managing multiple tumor types.
subtypes.frequency: Incidence of approximately 1 in 25,000–33,000 births. De novo mutations account for approximately 50% of cases [17].
key_associations: Bilateral vestibular schwannomas (defining feature in most cases)
NF2 gene mutations on chromosome 22q12
Bevacizumab for progressive VS: hearing improvement in 36%, stabilization in 46%, deterioration in 18% [3]
Partial VS volume reduction (≥20%) on MRI in 40% of patients treated with bevacizumab [3]
Brigatinib for progressive tumors [3]
Increased lifetime risk of CNS malignancy (~6%) after radiation [16]

gliomas.IDH1_and_IDH2_mutation: IDH1 and IDH2 are metabolic enzymes. Specific mutations (most commonly IDH1 R132H) lead to aberrant production of D-2-hydroxyglutarate (2-HG), an oncometabolite that causes epigenetic modifications [9]. IDH1 R132H is reliably screened by mutation-specific IHC; if negative, sequencing of IDH1 and IDH2 is recommended to detect less common mutations [BRAIN-E 2 of 10]. IDH mutations define WHO grade 2 and 3 astrocytomas and oligodendrogliomas, and grade 4 IDH-mutant astrocytomas. Their presence distinguishes lower-grade gliomas from glioblastomas (IDH wild-type) [12,18]. Prior to age 55, sequencing is required if R132H immunostain is negative or if glioma is grade 2 or 3 histologically.
gliomas.1p19q_codeletion: Represents unbalanced chromosome translocation t(1;19)(q10;p10) leading to whole-arm deletion of 1p and 19q [22]. Strongly associated with oligodendroglial histology [23]. Detectable by array-based genomic copy number testing (preferable) or FISH. IDH-mutant gliomas without loss of ATRX should be strongly considered for 1p/19q testing even if not clearly oligodendroglial by histology. IDH wild-type gliomas do not contain true whole-arm 1p/19q codeletion, so testing is unnecessary in definitely IDH wild-type tumors [24]. Confers favorable prognosis and predicts response to alkylating chemotherapy [26,27].
gliomas.TERT_promoter_mutation: TERT encodes telomerase, responsible for maintaining telomere length. Mutations in the noncoding promoter region cause increased TERT protein expression [41]. Nearly always present in 1p/19q codeleted oligodendrogliomas and found in most glioblastomas [28,29]. Absence of TERT promoter mutation coupled with IDH mutation strongly suggests astrocytoma. In IDH-mutant, 1p/19q codeleted oligodendrogliomas, absence of TERT promoter mutation is associated with worse OS (HR, 2.72; 95% CI, 1.05–7.04; P = .04) [49].
gliomas.ATRX_mutation: Encodes a chromatin regulator protein; loss-of-function mutations enable alternative lengthening of telomeres [37]. Strongly associated with IDH mutations and nearly always mutually exclusive with 1p/19q codeletion [38,39]. ATRX deficiency coupled with IDH1/2 mutation and TP53 mutation is typical of astrocytoma. Detection by IHC for loss of wild-type expression and/or sequencing [38].
gliomas.MGMT_promoter_methylation: MGMT encodes a DNA repair enzyme that reverses alkylation damage, causing tumor resistance to temozolomide and nitrosourea-based therapy. Methylation of the MGMT promoter silences MGMT expression, making tumors more sensitive to alkylating agents [28]. MGMT promoter methylation is strongly associated with IDH1/2 mutations and genome-wide epigenetic changes [4]. Confers a survival advantage in glioblastoma and is used for risk stratification in clinical trials [33]. Particularly useful in treatment decisions for older adult patients with high-grade gliomas [34,35]. Tested by methylation-specific PCR (most validated), pyrosequencing [72,73].
gliomas.EGFR_amplification: Found in a subset of glioblastomas. Combined with gain of chromosome 7, loss of chromosome 10, and/or TERT promoter mutation, can define glioblastoma diagnosis even without histologic grade 4 features [22,24].
gliomas.H3_3A_mutation: H3K27M mutation (lysine-to-methionine substitution in H3-3A gene) inhibits trimethylation of H3.3 histone [34,35]. Most common histone mutation in brain tumors. Diffuse midline gliomas should be screened for this mutation. H3K27M gliomas typically do not have MGMT promoter methylation and have poor prognosis. G34V/R mutation occurs in cerebral hemisphere gliomas in children and younger adults, IDH wild-type but ATRX and TP53 mutant [6]. When diagnosis of DMG H3 K27-altered is considered, CSF-tDNA testing should be considered [BRAIN-E 4 of 10].
gliomas.BRAF_mutation_and_fusion: Activating BRAF V600E mutation found in 60%–80% of PXAs, 30% of DNETs, 20% of gangliogliomas, 5% of PAs, and <5% of glioblastomas [57-62]. BRAF fusions (KIAA1549-BRAF) are typical of pilocytic astrocytomas, especially posterior fossa [57-59]. BRAF V600E tumors may respond to BRAF inhibitors [54,60,61]. Tumors with BRAF fusions tend to be indolent with occasional recurrence but rarely progress to lethality [59,60].
gliomas.CDKN2A_B_deletion: Homozygous deletion of CDKN2A/B on 9p21 is now considered evidence of grade 4 status in IDH-mutant astrocytomas even without necrosis or microvascular proliferation [6,23,30-33]. Strong adverse prognostic factor in diffuse malignant IDH-mutant gliomas [30].
gliomas.glioblastoma_IDH_wildtype_replication_re: Approximately 2% of glioblastomas have tumors that are hypermutated and DNA replication repair deficient. These tumors often have giant cell variant features or are classified as 'diffuse pediatric-type high-grade glioma, RTK1 subtype, subclass A' by DNA methylation array. Detected by TMB-H with biallelic inactivation of canonical mismatch repair genes (MSH2, MSH6, MLH1, or PMS2). May be sporadic or associated with Lynch syndrome; germline testing recommended. May benefit from immune checkpoint blockade [BRAIN-E 5 of 10].

ependymomas.ZFTA_fusion: Activating fusions of ZFTA (most common partner: RELA) occur in supratentorial ependymomas, causing increased NF-kappa-B signaling and more aggressive behavior. RELA activating fusions in approximately 19% of ependymomas [220]. More common in children than adults; occurs only in supratentorial location. Detected by RNA sequencing or break-apart FISH [71]. Fusion-positive ependymomas are a distinct WHO entity with worse PFS and OS [1,69,70,72].
ependymomas.MYCN_amplification: Spinal ependymomas with MYCN gene amplification behave more aggressively; now designated as SP-EPN-MYCN subtype in the 2021 WHO classification [1,222,223].
ependymomas.PFA_vs_PFB: Posterior fossa ependymomas categorized as PFA (more aggressive, associated with H3K27 trimethylation loss) and PFB. Genomic methylation profiling recommended for differentiation [24,218,224,63-68].

medulloblastoma.WNT_activated: WNT-driven tumors contain mutations in CTNNB1 (somatic) or APC (germline, Turcot syndrome) [259,268]. Monosomy 6 is a specific marker for pediatric WNT but not adult WNT [75]. Markedly better prognosis regardless of age, though WNT tumors may have worse prognosis in adults compared to children based on retrospective data [75]. Nuclear beta-catenin staining can be focal and may be misinterpreted.
medulloblastoma.SHH_activated: TP53 mutation detection is important: TP53-mutant tumors have more aggressive behavior, often in setting of germline TP53 variants [269,77]. SHH-activated TP53-wildtype tumors are uncommon in adults. Best classified by DNA methylation arrays or IHC panel (beta-catenin, GAB1, YAP1). Vismodegib and sonidegib showed activity against recurrent SHH-activated medulloblastoma [296,297].
medulloblastoma.non_WNT_non_SHH: Variable course; 6q loss not confined to WNT in adults, also described in SHH and Group 4 [75].

meningiomas.chromosomal_alterations: Concomitant loss of 22q and 1p sufficient to assign WHO grade 2. Two or more whole-arm losses affecting chromosomes 6, 10, 14, or 18 suggest higher risk tumors. Homozygous loss of CDKN2A/B or TERT promoter mutation justifies WHO grade 3 assignment [84]. Any validated assay measuring genome-wide copy number variation may be used; targeted assays like FISH are not recommended [BRAIN-E 7 of 10].
meningiomas.methylation_profiling: DNA methylation profiling adds prognostic value beyond standard histopathologic grading [84-86]. Multiple classifiers based on methylation patterns exist [84,86]. May differentiate ambiguous cases from other mesenchymal-type dural tumors [BRAIN-E 7 of 10].

epidemiology.incidence: In the United States, an estimated 25,050 people will be diagnosed with malignant primary CNS tumors annually, responsible for approximately 18,280 deaths [1]. Brain metastases are the most common intracranial tumors in adults, occurring up to 10 times more frequently than primary brain tumors [443-445]. About 8%–10% of patients with cancer are affected by symptomatic metastatic brain tumors [443,444]; autopsy studies show brain metastases in 25% of cancer patients [445]. Meningiomas: annual incidence 1.8/100,000 in males and 3.4/100,000 in females [408]. Primary spinal cord tumors: overall incidence 0.74 per 100,000 person-years [388]. Ependymomas constitute up to 1.6% of CNS tumors [74]. Primary CNS lymphoma: overall incidence 0.47 per 100,000 person-years in immunocompetent patients [301].
epidemiology.mortality: Approximately 18,280 deaths annually from malignant primary CNS tumors in the US [1]. Glioblastoma 5-year survival rates around 6%, with higher rates among younger age groups [74]. Primary spinal cord tumors: 10-year survival rate of 64% [388].
epidemiology.trends: Survival for CNS cancers has largely improved in recent decades, though less improvement has been observed in older adults, due to higher incidence of glioblastoma in this population [2]. Diagnosis of CNS involvement is increasing in patients with breast cancer as therapy for metastatic disease is improving [449]. Leptomeningeal metastasis is being diagnosed with increasing frequency as patients with cancer live longer [579].
epidemiology.demographics: PCNSL: higher incidence in males than females; incidence increases with age, with 1.8 per 100,000 in patients aged ≥65 years and 1.9 in patients aged ≥75 years [301,302]. Meningiomas have female predominance. Low-grade gliomas peak in 30–40 age range. Glioblastoma incidence increases significantly after age 50. Adult medulloblastoma is genetically distinct from pediatric medulloblastoma with differing prognostic markers [264].

risk_factors.detail: Prior cranial radiation therapy is a well-established risk factor for development of secondary CNS tumors, including meningiomas and gliomas. Radiation therapy for prior cancer is the most consistently identified risk factor for meningiomas. NF2-SWN patients have increased lifetime risk of CNS malignancy (~6%) in irradiated patients [16].
risk_factors.strength: Strong evidence from epidemiologic studies and clinical series

risk_factors.detail: Risk of CNS tumors increases with age, particularly for glioblastoma and meningiomas. Older adults have higher incidence of glioblastoma and less improvement in survival outcomes [2]. Gliomas are less common before age 30 (except pilocytic astrocytomas in children). IDH mutation is associated with younger age at diagnosis.

NF2 (neurofibromatosis type 2): bilateral vestibular schwannomas, multiple meningiomas, ependymomas. Li-Fraumeni syndrome (TP53 germline mutations): predisposition to various CNS tumors including gliomas. Tuberous sclerosis complex: SEGAs. VHL syndrome: hemangioblastomas. Lynch syndrome: DNA mismatch repair deficient glioblastomas. Gorlin syndrome (PTCH1): medulloblastoma (SHH subtype). Turcot syndrome (APC mutations): medulloblastoma (WNT subtype) [BRAIN-F].
risk_factors.strength: Strong evidence for all listed syndromes

risk_factors.detail: HIV/AIDS and iatrogenic immunosuppression (post-transplant) increase risk of PCNSL. PCNSL occurs in about 7%–15% of patients with post-transplant lymphoproliferative disorders [309-312]. AIDS-related PCNSL has a distinct clinical course with worse prognosis [303].
Higher incidence of primary CNS lymphoma in males than females in immunocompetent patients [301].
Strongly associated with PCNSL in immunocompromised patients (HIV/AIDS and post-transplant). EBV-positive PCNSL is virtually restricted to immunosuppressed individuals.

protective_factors: MGMT promoter methylation status (predictive of better response to temozolomide-based therapy in glioblastoma, not a causal protective factor but improves treatment outcomes)
IDH1/2 mutation status (associated with relatively favorable prognosis in grade 2–3 gliomas and better survival across treatment modalities)

clinical_features.typical_presentation: CNS tumors present with highly variable symptoms depending on tumor type, location, grade, and rate of growth. Primary brain tumors such as gliomas, medulloblastomas, and CNS lymphomas typically present with focal neurologic deficits, seizures, headache from increased intracranial pressure, cognitive or personality changes, and signs of increased intracranial pressure (papilledema, nausea/vomiting, altered mental status). Brain metastases may be found incidentally on screening MRI without any symptoms, or present similarly with focal neurologic deficits and increased intracranial pressure [MS-45]. Leptomeningeal metastases present with cranial nerve palsies, spinal cord symptoms, or hydrocephalus signs [MS-47]. Meningiomas are often incidental findings on imaging, but may present with seizures (27%) or focal neurologic symptoms related to location and mass effect [MS-34]. Spinal cord tumors present with progressive pain (often worse at night for intramedullary lesions), motor weakness, sensory loss, and late autonomic dysfunction [MS-32]. Sellar tumors (pituitary adenomas) present with hormonal syndromes or visual field defects from optic chiasm compression [SELLAR-1]. NF2-related schwannomatosis presents with bilateral vestibular schwannomas causing progressive hearing loss, as well as multiple meningiomas, ependymomas, and schwannomas throughout the neuraxis [BRAIN-G]. In a retrospective review of 248 immunocompetent patients with PCNSL, 43% had mental status changes, 33% showed signs of elevated intracranial pressure, 14% had seizures, and 4% suffered visual symptoms at diagnosis [MS-308].

symptoms.name: Seizures
symptoms.detail: Common presenting symptom for low-grade gliomas (81%), particularly oligodendrogliomas. Also occur in 14% of PCNSL and 27% of meningiomas. May be the sole presenting complaint in slowly growing tumors.
symptoms.frequency: 81% in low-grade gliomas; 14% in PCNSL; 27% in meningiomas
symptoms.alarm: New-onset seizure in an adult warrants brain MRI to evaluate for underlying structural lesion

symptoms.name: Headache
symptoms.detail: Occurs with increased intracranial pressure from mass effect or hydrocephalus. May be worse in the morning, exacerbated by Valsalva maneuver. Persistent progressive headaches in adults are an alarming symptom.
symptoms.frequency: Variable; common with posterior fossa tumors and hydrocephalus
symptoms.alarm: New persistent headache with neurologic symptoms or papilledema requires urgent evaluation

symptoms.name: Focal neurologic deficits
symptoms.detail: Hemiparesis, aphasia, visual field deficits, cranial nerve palsies depending on tumor location. Progressive motor weakness occurs in half of primary spinal cord tumor patients. Cranial nerve palsies may indicate leptomeningeal disease or skull base tumors.
symptoms.frequency: Variable; progressive in high-grade and metastatic tumors
symptoms.alarm: Acute or rapidly progressive focal deficits suggest hemorrhage into tumor, herniation, or acute cord compression

symptoms.name: Cognitive and personality changes
symptoms.detail: Mental status changes, memory loss, executive dysfunction, and personality changes are common with frontal or temporal lobe tumors. Up to 90% of individuals with supratentorial brain tumors experience some degree of neurocognitive dysfunction [MS-19-21]. In PCNSL, 43% present with mental status changes.
symptoms.frequency: Up to 90% of supratentorial brain tumor patients; 43% of PCNSL patients
symptoms.alarm: Rapid cognitive decline may indicate tumor progression, hydrocephalus, or leptomeningeal disease

symptoms.name: Hearing loss
symptoms.detail: Unilateral or bilateral progressive hearing loss is the hallmark of vestibular schwannomas. In NF2-SWN, bilateral VS causes progressive hearing loss; treatment with bevacizumab results in hearing improvement in 36% of patients [BRAIN-G]. Sudden sensorineural hearing loss may occur.
symptoms.frequency: Most common symptom in NF2-SWN vestibular schwannomas
symptoms.alarm: Unilateral or progressive hearing loss requires audiologic evaluation and MRI

symptoms.name: Visual disturbances
symptoms.detail: Visual field deficits from optic pathway compression by pituitary adenomas or craniopharyngiomas. Ocular involvement in PCNSL occurs independently in 10%-20% of patients, presenting as blurred vision or floaters. Pituitary adenomas can cause bitemporal hemianopsia.
symptoms.frequency: Visual impairment from pituitary tumors; 10%-20% ocular involvement in PCNSL
symptoms.alarm: New visual loss or visual field defect requires urgent ophthalmologic evaluation

symptoms.name: Motor weakness and sensory loss
symptoms.detail: Progressive motor weakness occurs in half of primary spinal cord tumor patients. Spinal cord compression from metastatic spine tumors presents with progressive radicular pain followed by motor weakness, sensory loss, and bowel/bladder dysfunction.
symptoms.frequency: 50% progressive motor weakness in spinal cord tumors
symptoms.alarm: Acute onset of weakness or bowel/bladder dysfunction indicates cord compression emergency

symptoms.name: Pain
symptoms.detail: Three classic types: local pain (constant, deep aching improving with steroids), mechanical pain (varies with movement, indicates instability), and radicular pain (sharp/stabbing from nerve root compression). Pain worsening at night is classic for intramedullary lesions.
symptoms.frequency: Primary symptom preceding neurologic dysfunction in most spinal metastases
symptoms.alarm: New severe back pain with neurologic symptoms requires urgent spine MRI

symptoms.name: Gait instability and ataxia
symptoms.detail: Cerebellar tumors (medulloblastoma, posterior fossa metastases) cause truncal and limb ataxia, dysmetria, and gait instability. Posterior fossa ependymomas can cause neck stiffness and head tilt.
symptoms.alarm: Progressive ataxia with signs of hydrocephalus requires urgent evaluation

signs.name: Papilledema
signs.detail: Elevated intracranial pressure from mass lesions, hydrocephalus, or diffuse disease. Found on fundoscopic examination.
signs.frequency: Common with larger tumors and hydrocephalus

signs.name: Motor deficits
signs.detail: Upper motor neuron signs (hyperreflexia, Babinski sign, spasticity) from cortical or corticospinal tract compression. Spinal cord compression may show motor weakness, hyperreflexia below the level of compression.
signs.frequency: Common in high-grade gliomas and spinal cord tumors

signs.name: Cranial nerve palsies
signs.detail: May indicate skull base tumor, leptomeningeal metastases, or nerve sheath tumors. Cranial nerve VIII dysfunction (hearing loss, tinnitus, vestibular symptoms) is hallmark of vestibular schwannomas. New cranial nerve palsies in the context of known cancer suggest leptomeningeal disease.
signs.frequency: Common in leptomeningeal metastases; 100% of VS in NF2

signs.name: Cerebellar signs
signs.detail: Intention tremor, dysdiadochokinesia, nystagmus, gait ataxia with posterior fossa tumors.

signs.name: Sensory deficits
signs.detail: Sensory level on examination may help localize spinal cord compression. Stocking-glove sensory loss is excluded from abnormal examination for spinal cord compression assessment.

signs.name: Endocrine dysfunction
signs.detail: Hypopituitarism, hyperprolactinemia, Cushing disease, acromegaly, diabetes insipidus from sellar/parasellar tumors. Endocrinopathies are common in brain tumor patients and may be affected by steroid use, RT, and surgery.
signs.frequency: Common in sellar tumors; general in brain tumor patients

atypical_or_missed: Asymptomatic brain metastases found on screening MRI without any neurologic symptoms, which may be increasing with improved detection sensitivity of modern MRI [MS-45]
Ocular involvement in PCNSL presenting as uveitis or blurred vision without brain parenchymal symptoms, potentially missed if ophthalmologic exam not performed [MS-30]
Leptomeningeal disease presenting with isolated cranial neuropathy or hydrocephalus without classic meningeal signs
Low-grade gliomas presenting solely with new-onset seizure with no focal deficits, delaying diagnosis by median 6-17 months [MS-7]
Meningiomas discovered as incidental findings on imaging for unrelated reasons, growing slowly without symptoms for years [MS-34]
Spinal cord metastases with pain attributed to degenerative disease, delaying MRI diagnosis by median 3 months for initial pain and 2 months for diagnosis [MS-615]
NF2-SWN with subclinical bilateral vestibular schwannomas detected only on MRI surveillance rather than symptoms
Pituitary apoplexy presenting acutely with sudden headache, visual loss, and ophthalmoplegia from hemorrhage or infarction of pituitary adenoma
Pediatric-type diffuse midline gliomas (H3K27M-mutant) in adults presenting with brainstem symptoms that may be attributed to other conditions
Pseudoprogression mimicking tumor progression on MRI in the first 3 months after RT, leading to unnecessary treatment changes [MS-144]

red_flags: New-onset seizure in an adult without prior history, especially with focal neurologic symptoms
Progressive headache with papilledema, nausea, or vomiting suggesting increased intracranial pressure
Acute or rapidly progressive focal neurologic deficits (hemiparesis, aphasia, visual loss) suggesting mass effect, hemorrhage, or herniation
Signs of spinal cord compression: progressive weakness, sensory level, bowel/bladder dysfunction — spinal cord compression is a medical emergency requiring intervention within 24 hours [MS-54]
Rapid cognitive decline or acute altered mental status suggesting hydrocephalus, leptomeningeal disease, or tumor hemorrhage
New cranial nerve palsies in a patient with known cancer, suggesting leptomeningeal metastases
Acute severe back pain with new neurologic deficits in a cancer patient, requiring urgent spine MRI [MS-54]
Sudden visual loss or bitemporal hemianopsia suggesting pituitary tumor with acute apoplexy
Bilateral vestibular schwannomas identified on imaging, indicating NF2-SWN and requiring comprehensive workup [NF2-SWN-1]
Pituitary adenoma with acute deterioration suggesting apoplexy (hemorrhage or infarction)
Any new or worsening neurologic symptoms in a patient with known cancer and brain metastases, suggesting progression, hemorrhage, or leptomeningeal spread
Tumor bleeding (hemorrhagic conversion): acute sudden-onset severe headache with neurologic deterioration, particularly with melanoma, renal cell carcinoma, or choriocarcinoma metastases

diagnostic.test: Brain MRI with and without gadolinium contrast
diagnostic.rationale: Standard of care imaging method for all CNS neoplasms, providing superior soft tissue contrast for detection and characterization. Required at diagnosis, pre-treatment baseline, and post-treatment surveillance [BRAIN-A, MS-2]
diagnostic.key_findings: Gliomas: infiltrative lesions with variable enhancement (low-grade often non-enhancing, high-grade typically enhancing with necrosis); Brain metastases: ring-enhancing lesions at gray-white junction; Meningiomas: dural-based homogeneously enhancing mass with dural tail; PCNSL: periventricular enhancing lesions, restricted diffusion; Medulloblastoma: enhancing heterogeneous posterior fossa mass

diagnostic.rationale: Required for staging of medulloblastoma, ependymomas, PCNSL, and leptomeningeal metastases. Must be performed before lumbar puncture to avoid false-positive imaging artifacts. Should be delayed 2-3 weeks postoperatively to avoid artifacts [BRAIN-A, MS-24]
diagnostic.key_findings: Drop metastases, leptomeningeal enhancement, intramedullary spinal cord tumors, extradural compression from spinal metastases

diagnostic.rationale: Alternative when MRI contraindicated; shows calcifications in meningiomas (25%) and ependymomas, bone changes, acute hemorrhage, and hydrocephalus [BRAIN-A, MS-34]
diagnostic.key_findings: Meningioma: hyperostosis of surrounding skull, calcification; Acute hemorrhage into tumor; Ventricular enlargement

diagnostic.rationale: Required for histopathologic diagnosis when surgery not feasible. Strongly recommended as the primary procedure for PCNSL diagnosis [PCNS-1]. Stereotactic biopsy is preferred over resection for PCNSL to minimize invasiveness [MS-318]. Frozen section analysis helps guide intraoperative decision-making [BRAIN-B]
diagnostic.key_findings: Histopathologic grade, molecular markers essential for integrated diagnosis per WHO 2021 classification

diagnostic.test: Surgical resection (gross total resection or maximal safe resection)
diagnostic.rationale: Primary therapeutic and diagnostic modality for gliomas, ependymomas, meningiomas, medulloblastoma, and spinal cord tumors. Goals include maximal safe resection, symptom relief, and adequate tissue for pathology [BRAIN-B, MS-4]. Extent of resection should be documented with postoperative MRI within 48 hours.
diagnostic.key_findings: Extent of resection (GTR vs. STR vs. biopsy), tissue for molecular characterization

diagnostic.rationale: Systemic workup for brain metastases when primary unknown; staging evaluation for PCNSL to exclude systemic involvement [MS-45, PCNS-2]
diagnostic.key_findings: Primary tumor identification, systemic disease extent

diagnostic.rationale: Considered for staging of brain metastases when primary unknown; for PCNSL staging to evaluate systemic involvement [PCNS-2]. Whole-body PET/CT may be considered for patients with brain metastases of unknown origin.

Essential for staging medulloblastoma (cytology, cell count), ependymoma (dissemination assessment), PCNSL (diagnostic adjunct when tissue biopsy not feasible), and leptomeningeal metastases (cytology positive for tumor cells). Must be done after spine MRI; delayed 2 weeks post-surgery to avoid false-positive cytology [MS-24, PCNS-1]. CSF analysis should include flow cytometry, cytology, cell count, PCR or NGS of MYD88, and possibly IgH gene rearrangement for PCNSL [PCNS-1]. When available, CSF-tDNA testing can increase sensitivity [BRAIN-E]
diagnostic.key_findings: PCNSL: positive cytology, monoclonal lymphocytes, MYD88 mutation; Leptomeningeal metastases: positive cytology, elevated protein, decreased glucose, pleocytosis; Medulloblastoma/ependymoma: tumor cells indicating neuraxis dissemination

diagnostic.test: Full ophthalmologic exam including slit-lamp examination
diagnostic.rationale: Required for PCNSL evaluation to detect vitreoretinal involvement which occurs independently in 10%-20% of patients [PCNS-2, MS-30]. Diagnostic vitrectomy may be performed for primary vitreoretinal lymphoma.
diagnostic.key_findings: Vitreous cells, retinal infiltrates, optic nerve involvement

diagnostic.test: Lumbar puncture for CSF flow study
diagnostic.rationale: Indicated before intrathecal chemotherapy in leptomeningeal metastases if CSF flow blockage suspected. Flow abnormalities require radiation correction before intrathecal therapy [LEPT-2]
diagnostic.key_findings: CSF flow obstruction requiring treatment before intrathecal chemotherapy

diagnostic.rationale: Required for PCNSL evaluation; both prognosis and treatment differ in HIV-related PCNSL. HIV-positive patients should receive antiretroviral therapy with cancer treatment [PCNS-2, MS-30]
diagnostic.key_findings: HIV status affects prognosis and treatment approach

diagnostic.rationale: Category 2B option for PCNSL staging if clinical suspicion of bone marrow involvement [PCNS-2]. May also be considered in metastatic workup of brain metastases from unknown primary.
diagnostic.key_findings: Bone marrow involvement by lymphoma or metastatic disease

staging.test: Brain and spine MRI (complete neuraxis imaging)
staging.rationale: Required for staging medulloblastoma, ependymoma, PCNSL, and leptomeningeal metastases. Shows extent of local disease, multifocal disease, and CSF dissemination along the neuraxis [AMED-2, EPEN-2, PCNS-2, LEPT-1]
staging.indication: Medulloblastoma: brain and spine MRI for Chang staging; Ependymoma: brain and spine MRI for metastasis assessment; PCNSL: full CNS evaluation including spine MRI; LM: staging of neuroaxis

staging.rationale: Systemic staging for brain metastases to identify primary tumor and assess systemic disease burden [LTD-1, MU-1]. For PCNSL, contrast-enhanced body CT or PET/CT required to rule out systemic involvement [PCNS-2]
staging.indication: All patients with brain metastases of unknown primary; PCNSL staging; bone scan or whole body FDG-PET/CT if clinically indicated

staging.rationale: Added as follow-up imaging for meningiomas every 3-6 months per updated recommendations [BRAIN-A]. SSTR PET/CT or PET/MRI may help distinguish meningioma from dural metastases and improve radiation planning [BRAIN-A].
staging.indication: Meningioma: follow-up imaging and treatment planning; Consider when equivocal on standard MRI

biomarkers.rationale: Required for workup of ALL gliomas per WHO 2021 classification. IDH1 R132H most common mutation, detected by IHC; sequencing required for non-canonical mutations. IDH mutations define WHO grade 2-4 astrocytomas and oligodendrogliomas, and distinguish them from glioblastoma (IDH wild-type) [BRAIN-E, MS-4-5]
biomarkers.when: All gliomas at initial diagnosis

biomarkers.rationale: Essential for diagnosis of oligodendroglioma. A tumor should only be diagnosed as oligodendroglioma if it contains both IDH1/2 mutation AND 1p/19q codeletion [BRAIN-E, MS-7]. Detectable by array-based genomic copy number testing (preferable) or FISH.
biomarkers.when: All gliomas where oligodendroglioma is in the differential; IDH-mutant gliomas without ATRX loss should be strongly considered for 1p/19q testing

biomarkers.rationale: Essential for high-grade gliomas (grade 3 and 4). Predictive biomarker for response to alkylating chemotherapy. MGMT promoter methylated tumors respond better to temozolomide. Methods include methylation-specific PCR (most validated), pyrosequencing (possibly best prognostic stratifier), and droplet-digital PCR [BRAIN-E, MS-6, MS-33-35]
biomarkers.when: Grade 3 and 4 gliomas; glioblastoma

biomarkers.rationale: Required for glioma workup. ATRX mutations are strongly associated with IDH mutations and nearly always mutually exclusive with 1p/19q codeletion. Loss of nuclear ATRX immunostaining indicates mutation, supporting astrocytoma over oligodendroglioma diagnosis [BRAIN-E, MS-5]
biomarkers.when: All gliomas

biomarkers.rationale: Recommended for glioma workup. Nearly always present in IDH wild-type glioblastomas and IDH-mutant 1p/19q codeleted oligodendrogliomas. Combined with IDH and 1p/19q status, helps classify gliomas into molecular subtypes with distinct prognoses [BRAIN-E, MS-5-6]
biomarkers.when: All gliomas, particularly when classification is uncertain

biomarkers.rationale: Clinically indicated for low-grade gliomas where BRAF-targeted therapy may be appropriate. BRAF V600E found in 60%-80% of PXA, 30% of DNETs, 20% of gangliogliomas, 5% of pilocytic astrocytomas [BRAIN-E, MS-6]. BRAF fusions found in pilocytic astrocytomas. May guide treatment with dabrafenib/trametinib, vemurafenib/cobimetinib, or tovorafenib.
biomarkers.when: Low-grade gliomas; recurrent glioblastoma; when targeted therapy consideration

biomarkers.rationale: Recommended in appropriate clinical context for diffuse midline gliomas (H3K27M) and hemispheric gliomas (H3G34). H3K27M-mutant diffuse midline gliomas are WHO grade 4 with poor prognosis. CSF-tDNA testing should be considered for diagnosis and monitoring [BRAIN-E, MS-5]
biomarkers.when: Midline gliomas; suspected H3-mutated high-grade gliomas; pediatric-type diffuse high-grade gliomas

biomarkers.rationale: Preferred approach for pathologic workup of CNS tumors, screening for multiple diagnostic and prognostic mutations in one test. May identify BRAF V600E or NTRK fusions that could guide targeted therapy [BRAIN-E, MS-4].
biomarkers.when: All primary brain tumors; glioblastoma (encouraged); may inform clinical trial eligibility

biomarkers.rationale: Tumor-agnostic biomarker; patients with NTRK fusion-positive solid tumors may respond to TRK inhibitors (larotrectinib, entrectinib, repotrectinib) including for brain metastases [BRAIN-A, BRAIN METS-A]
biomarkers.when: Any brain tumor or brain metastasis when targeted therapy options are being considered

biomarkers.rationale: Tumor-agnostic markers; pembrolizumab is a category 2B preferred option for MSI-H/dMMR or TMB-H tumors with isolated brain metastases [BRAIN METS-A]
biomarkers.when: Isolated brain metastases when tumor-agnostic therapy is being considered

biomarkers.rationale: Relevant for NSCLC brain metastases; pembrolizumab recommended for PD-L1 TPS >=1%, nivolumab for TPS >=1% [BRAIN METS-A]
biomarkers.when: NSCLC brain metastases being considered for immunotherapy

biomarkers.rationale: Relevant for NSCLC brain metastases; adagrasib and sotorasib have activity [BRAIN METS-A]
biomarkers.when: NSCLC brain metastases
