biomarkers.rationale: Essential for NSCLC; second- and third-generation ALK inhibitors (brigatinib, lorlatinib, alectinib, ceritinib) have excellent CNS activity for brain metastases [BRAIN METS-A, MS-42]
biomarkers.when: All NSCLC at diagnosis, particularly for brain metastases

biomarkers.rationale: Essential for NSCLC; osimertinib has excellent CNS penetration. Also relevant for leptomeningeal metastases [BRAIN METS-A, LEPT-A]
biomarkers.when: All NSCLC at diagnosis, particularly for brain metastases and leptomeningeal disease

biomarkers.rationale: Relevant for NSCLC; crizotinib, repotrectinib, and taletrectinib have CNS activity [BRAIN METS-A]
biomarkers.when: NSCLC brain metastases

biomarkers.rationale: Essential for melanoma brain metastases; dabrafenib/trametinib, vemurafenib/cobimetinib have CNS activity [BRAIN METS-A, MS-40-41]
biomarkers.when: All melanoma with brain metastases

biomarkers.rationale: Essential for breast cancer brain metastases; HER2-directed therapies including tucatinib/trastuzumab/capecitabine have CNS activity [BRAIN METS-A, MS-44]
biomarkers.when: All breast cancer with brain metastases

biomarkers.rationale: For PCNSL diagnosis; PCR or NGS assays of MYD88 in CSF can help establish diagnosis when tissue biopsy not feasible [PCNS-1, MS-30]
biomarkers.when: Suspected PCNSL when stereotactic biopsy not feasible

biomarkers.rationale: When available, CSF-tDNA testing can be considered with CSF cytology to increase sensitivity of tumor cell detection and assessment of residual disease after surgery or induction therapy [BRAIN-E, LEPT-1]. For H3K27-altered tumors, CSF-tDNA testing should be further considered.
biomarkers.when: Ependymoma, medulloblastoma, PCNSL, leptomeningeal metastases; when available

biomarkers.rationale: Recommended for recurrent glioblastoma, particularly MGMT-promoter methylated recurrent GBMs. This signature renders the recurrent GBM resistant to further temozolomide [BRAIN-E]
biomarkers.when: Recurrent glioblastoma, especially MGMT-methylated

biomarkers.test: DNA mismatch repair protein testing (IHC and NGS)
biomarkers.rationale: Recommended for newly diagnosed glioblastoma with TMB-H to identify hypermutated/DNA replication repair deficient subtype. May predict benefit from immune checkpoint blockade. Patients should undergo germline testing for Lynch syndrome [BRAIN-E]
biomarkers.when: Newly diagnosed glioblastoma with TMB-H

biomarkers.rationale: For ependymoma: genomic methylation profiling is recommended for differentiation of PFA and PFB ependymomas [BRAIN-E]. For meningioma: may screen for genomic copy number variations; validated internally. For gliomas: genome-wide CpG methylation profiling is a powerful classification tool [BRAIN-E, MS-4]
biomarkers.when: Ependymoma; meningioma when histopathology ambiguous; glioma when classification uncertain

biomarkers.test: ZFTA and YAP1 gene fusion testing (ependymoma)
biomarkers.rationale: ZFTA gene fusions are found in supratentorial ependymomas with more aggressive behavior. YAP1 fusions have better prognosis. Testing recommended in appropriate clinical context [BRAIN-E, MS-21]
biomarkers.when: Supratentorial ependymomas

biomarkers.rationale: MYCN-amplified spinal ependymoma is an aggressive subtype now codified as SP-EPN-MYCN in WHO classification [BRAIN-E, MS-222-223]
biomarkers.when: Spinal ependymomas

biomarkers.rationale: Referred to academic centers with expertise. WNT-activated tumors have markedly better prognosis. SHH-activated with TP53 mutation has worse prognosis. DNA methylation arrays or IHC panel (beta-catenin, GAB1, YAP1) recommended [BRAIN-E, MS-263]
biomarkers.when: All medulloblastomas

biomarkers.rationale: Any validated assay measuring copy number variation across the entire genome. FISH not recommended (insufficient coverage). Homozygous CDKN2A/B loss or TERT promoter mutation justifies WHO grade 3 [BRAIN-E, MS-409]
biomarkers.when: Recurrent meningiomas, grade 1 borderline, grade 2-3, specific histologic patterns

do_not_miss: Postoperative brain MRI within 48 hours for gliomas and brain tumors to document extent of resection as baseline [BRAIN-B, MS-4]
Spine MRI before lumbar puncture to avoid false-positive imaging artifacts; delayed 2-3 weeks postoperatively [AMED-2, EPEN-2, MS-24]
IDH1/2 mutation testing for ALL gliomas — this is required, not optional [BRAIN-E]
1p/19q codeletion for any glioma where oligodendroglioma is in differential — requires both IDH mutation AND 1p/19q codeletion for diagnosis [BRAIN-E]
MGMT promoter methylation for grade 3-4 gliomas — essential for treatment planning [BRAIN-E, MS-33]
Molecular profiling for medulloblastoma to identify clinically relevant subtypes and inform prognosis [AMED-2]
Full ophthalmologic exam including slit-lamp examination for all PCNSL patients [PCNS-2]
HIV testing for all PCNSL patients [PCNS-2]
CSF analysis (cytology, flow cytometry, MYD88 PCR/NGS) for PCNSL when tissue biopsy not feasible [PCNS-1]
Whole body FDG-PET/CT or contrast-enhanced CT for brain metastases when primary unknown [LTD-1, MU-1]
Genomic methylation profiling for ependymoma to differentiate PFA and PFB subtypes [BRAIN-E]
Germline testing for Lynch syndrome in glioblastoma with DNA replication repair deficiency [BRAIN-E]
Multidisciplinary discussion strongly recommended for all newly diagnosed or recurrent gliomas [BRAIN-D, MS-2]
VHL screening for hemangioblastomas including neuraxis imaging [PSCT-2]
avoid: High-dose steroids prior to biopsy when PCNSL is suspected, as steroids can profoundly affect imaging and histologic features, reducing diagnostic yield [PCNS-1, MS-26-27]
Lumbar puncture when increased intracranial pressure with risk of herniation (e.g., posterior fossa mass) [EPEN-2, LEPT-1]
Lumbar puncture in patients who are anticoagulated, thrombocytopenic, or have bulky intracranial mass without careful risk-benefit assessment [PCNS-2, MS-30]
Routine seizure prophylaxis in asymptomatic patients — not recommended per guidelines [BRAIN-D]
Enzyme-inducing antiepileptic drugs (phenytoin, phenobarbital, carbamazepine) when systemic therapy is planned, as they affect cytochrome P450 metabolism [BRAIN-D]
FISH for BRAF gene fusions — too unreliable; RNA sequencing or PCR-based methods preferred [BRAIN-E]
CT as primary imaging modality for brain tumors when MRI is available and not contraindicated — MRI is superior [BRAIN-A]
Delayed diagnosis from attribution of spinal cord compression symptoms to degenerative disease in cancer patients
Routine WBRT addition to SRS for brain metastases — associated with cognitive deterioration and no OS benefit [MS-464-465, MS-497]
Continuing temozolomide beyond 6 cycles for glioblastoma — no clear data for benefit even in MGMT-methylated disease, associated with greater toxicity [MS-172-173]

staging.system: CNS tumors do not use AJCC TNM staging. Gliomas, ependymomas, medulloblastoma, and meningiomas are classified by WHO histologic and molecular grade (2021 WHO Classification of Tumors of the CNS). Brain metastases are classified as 'limited' versus 'extensive' based on treatability with SRS versus WBRT. Leptomeningeal metastases are stratified by risk (good vs. poor) rather than anatomic stage. Metastatic spine tumors are classified by anatomic location (extradural, intradural-extramedullary, intradural-intramedullary) and presence of spinal cord compression.

t_stages.description: CNS primary tumors do not use T staging. Tumors are classified by WHO histologic and molecular grade (Grade 1-4) and integrated molecular diagnosis per WHO 2021 classification.

n_stages.description: CNS primary tumors do not use N staging. Nodal disease is not applicable to primary brain tumors. For brain metastases, extent of disease is classified as limited vs. extensive.

m_stages.stage: Not applicable for primary CNS tumors
m_stages.description: Primary CNS tumors are staged by WHO grade, not M stage. Medulloblastoma uses modified Chang staging (M0 = no metastasis, M1 = CSF cytology positive, M2 = intracranial metastases beyond primary, M3 = spinal drop metastases, M4 = extraneural metastases). Leptomeningeal metastases represent disseminated disease within the CNS.

stage_groups.criteria: Pilocytic astrocytoma, subependymal giant cell astrocytoma (SEGA), ganglioglioma, dysembryoplastic neuroepithelial tumor (DNET). Noninvasive, surgically curable [GLIO-1, MS-7-8]
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Generally indolent, curable with surgery alone. SEGA associated with tuberous sclerosis complex.

stage_groups.criteria: IDH1/2-mutant with 1p/19q whole-arm codeletion. Non-enhancing or minimally enhancing on MRI [GLIO-2, MS-9]
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Relatively favorable prognosis among diffuse gliomas. Responds to RT and alkylating chemotherapy. 1p/19q codeletion and IDH mutation both confer favorable prognosis [MS-8, MS-13-14]
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Potentially long-term disease control; observation possible after GTR

stage_groups.criteria: IDH1/2-mutant without 1p/19q codeletion. No ATRX loss excluded. No CDKN2A/B homozygous deletion [GLIO-4, MS-9-10]
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Intermediate prognosis between oligodendroglioma and glioblastoma. Progresses faster than grade 2 oligodendroglioma. IDH mutation confers favorable prognosis [MS-8-9]
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Long-term disease control; observation possible after GTR in select cases

stage_groups.criteria: Higher-grade oligodendroglioma with IDH mutation and 1p/19q codeletion [GLIO-3, MS-14-15]
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Better prognosis than other grade 3-4 gliomas. Significant survival benefit from RT + PCV (EORTC 26951: OS not reached in RT+PCV vs 112 months RT alone in 1p19q codeleted subgroup) [MS-161-162]. RTOG 9402: doubling of median OS with PCV in 1p19q codeleted tumors (14.7 vs 7.3 years) [MS-163-164]
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Long-term disease control with RT + PCV

stage_groups.criteria: IDH1/2-mutant astrocytoma, grade 3 by histology [GLIO-5, MS-15-16]
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: CATNON trial showed benefit of adjuvant TMZ in IDH-mutant anaplastic gliomas (HR 0.48, P < .0001) [MS-167]. RT + adjuvant TMZ is preferred.

stage_groups.criteria: IDH1/2-mutant astrocytoma with necrosis/microvascular proliferation OR CDKN2A/B homozygous deletion, regardless of classic histologic features [GLIO-6, MS-6, MS-23]
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Prognosis better than IDH wild-type glioblastoma. CATNON trial benefit from adjuvant TMZ extrapolated. RT + concurrent and adjuvant TMZ is preferred [MS-140, MS-167]

stage_groups.criteria: Diffusely infiltrative astrocytoma with IDH wild-type status AND at least one of: necrosis, microvascular proliferation, EGFR amplification, gain of chr7/loss of chr10, or TERT promoter mutation [GLIO-10-11, MS-6, MS-22]
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Most common malignant brain tumor in adults. Very poor prognosis. Median OS 14.6 months with RT + TMZ (EORTC-NCIC) [MS-160]. MGMT promoter methylation is both prognostic and predictive for TMZ benefit. Alternating electric field therapy improves survival in good PS patients [MS-178-179]

stage_groups.criteria: IDH wild-type glioblastoma in patients older than 70 [GLIO-11, MS-16-17]
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Hypofractionated RT + concurrent and adjuvant TMZ preferred for MGMT-methylated tumors. Hypofractionated RT alone is a category 2B option. TMZ alone may be reasonable for MGMT-methylated tumors in elderly who prefer to delay radiation [MS-54-55, MS-141]

stage_groups.criteria: Posterior fossa ependymoma with loss of H3K27 trimethylation by IHC, confirmed by genomic methylation profiling [EPEN-2, MS-21, BRAIN-E]
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: More aggressive behavior than PFB subtype. Standard RT recommended regardless of extent of resection for intracranial PFA ependymomas [EPEN-2]

stage_groups.criteria: No evidence of metastasis, small-volume residual disease (<1.5 cm2), classic or desmoplastic histology [AMED-2]
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: WNT-activated subtype has best prognosis. Molecular profiling recommended to inform prognosis [AMED-2, MS-259]

stage_groups.criteria: Unresectable tumor, residual >1.5 cm2, disseminated disease, or large cell histology [AMED-2]
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Requires neuraxis irradiation and systemic therapy. SHH-activated TP53-mutant has particularly poor prognosis [MS-269]
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Long-term disease control with aggressive multimodality

stage_groups.criteria: Aggressive non-Hodgkin lymphoma within brain, spine, CSF, or leptomeninges without systemic involvement [PCNS-1, MS-301-305]
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: High-dose methotrexate-based regimens are standard. Complete response to chemotherapy ranges 42%-61% with OS 14-55 months [MS-320-323]. High-dose chemotherapy with stem cell rescue feasible for fitter patients. Approximately 50% of initial complete responders eventually relapse [MS-328]
10-year PFS 75% after GTR, 39% after STR [MS-418]. Often curable with complete resection.
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative with GTR; observation for small asymptomatic

stage_groups.criteria: Atypical meningioma; 6% of all meningiomas [MS-409]. Concomitant loss of 22q and 1p sufficient for grade 2 [BRAIN-E]
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Higher recurrence risk (20%-41%) even after GTR [MS-419-420]. Postoperative RT recommended for incomplete resection; may be considered after GTR [MENI-1]

stage_groups.criteria: Malignant meningioma; 2% of all meningiomas. Homozygous CDKN2A/B loss or TERT promoter mutation justifies grade 3 [BRAIN-E, MS-409]
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Highest recurrence risk (56%-63%) [MS-421]. Adjuvant RT recommended regardless of extent of resection [MENI-1]. Postoperative RT 59.4-60 Gy in 1.8-2.0 Gy fractions [BRAIN-C]

stage_groups.criteria: Patients for whom SRS is an effective alternative to WBRT with more cognitive protection. Definition evolving based on number of metastases and intracranial disease volume [LTD-1, MS-467]
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: SRS is preferred treatment. Cognitive preservation superior to WBRT [MS-464-465]. Multiple lesions with low total volume may still be candidates for SRS [MS-467-471]
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: Highly variable by primary tumor type and systemic disease status

stage_groups.criteria: Metastases not fitting definition of 'limited' — too numerous or too large for SRS, or widespread disease [MU-1, MS-46-47]
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: WBRT with or without HA + memantine is primary treatment. SRS may be considered in select patients. Systemic therapy may be appropriate [MU-1]
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: Generally poor; highly variable by primary

stage_groups.criteria: KPS >=60, no major neurologic deficits, minimal systemic disease, reasonable systemic treatment options [LEPT-1, MS-48]
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Chemotherapy (systemic or intrathecal) recommended. May also receive RT to symptomatic sites. Median survival typically 2-4 months but may be extended [MS-47-48, MS-580-581]

stage_groups.criteria: KPS <60, multiple serious neurologic deficits, extensive systemic disease with few options, bulky CNS disease, encephalopathy [LEPT-1, MS-48]
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Palliative/supportive care. RT to symptomatic sites for temporary improvement [LEPT-2]

staging_pearls: CNS tumors do not use AJCC TNM staging; WHO grade and integrated molecular diagnosis are the primary classification systems per WHO 2021 [MS-3-6]

For gliomas, the combination of IDH status, 1p/19q codeletion, and TERT promoter mutation divides gliomas into three major molecular subtypes with distinct prognoses: oligodendroglioma (IDH+1p/19q+) > astrocytoma (IDH+1p/19q-) > glioblastoma (IDH wild-type) [MS-8-10]
IDH mutation in a grade 2-3 glioma indicates at least a grade 2 diffusely infiltrative glioma; true grade 1 non-infiltrative gliomas do not have IDH mutations [BRAIN-E, MS-22]
Glioblastoma is now reserved exclusively for IDH wild-type tumors with traditional histologic features or molecular hallmarks of glioblastoma [MS-6, MS-22-23]
The term 'oligoastrocytoma' should no longer be used; molecular testing resolves morphologically ambiguous tumors into astrocytomas or oligodendrogliomas [BRAIN-E, MS-17, MS-25]
For brain metastases, total volume of intracranial disease and rate of developing new metastases may be more important prognostic factors than number of discrete lesions [MS-468-471]
Modified Chang staging is used for medulloblastoma, not standard CNS tumor grading [AMED-2, MS-298-299]
For ependymomas, posterior fossa type A (PFA) and type B (PFB) distinction by methylation profiling is critical for prognosis and treatment planning [BRAIN-E, EPEN-2, MS-21]
CDKN2A/B homozygous deletion is now sufficient for grade 4 designation in IDH-mutant astrocytoma even without classic histologic features of glioblastoma [BRAIN-E, MS-6, MS-30-33]

For PCNSL, a positive CSF cytology in solid tumors is virtually always diagnostic, but CSF cytology has approximately 50% sensitivity with first lumbar puncture, up to 90% after repeated analyses [MS-585-586]
Pseudoprogression occurs in the first 3 months or longer after RT, more often in MGMT-methylated tumors, and must be distinguished from true tumor progression [MS-144, MS-200-201]

Spinal cord compression is a medical emergency; intervention should be implemented immediately to prevent irreversible neurologic damage. Paraplegia for over 24 hours is a strong relative contraindication to surgery [MS-613-615, MS-617]
For NF2-SWN, the diagnosis is confirmed by bilateral vestibular schwannomas OR 2 major criteria OR 1 major + 2 minor criteria [NF2-SWN-1, BRAIN-G]

management_principles.overview: The management of primary and metastatic central nervous system (CNS) tumors requires a highly individualized, multidisciplinary approach due to the heterogeneity of tumor types, locations, and patient factors. Treatment goals vary from curative intent (e.g., for low-grade circumscribed gliomas) to prolonging survival and maintaining quality of life (e.g., for glioblastoma or leptomeningeal metastases). A multidisciplinary tumor board, ideally comprising neurosurgeons, radiation oncologists, neuro-oncologists, neuropathologists, neuro-radiologists, and allied health services (physical, occupational, speech therapy; neuropsychology; social work), is strongly recommended for all patients [BRAIN-D 1-6]. Clinical trial participation is encouraged whenever possible for all patients, as standard treatments for many CNS tumors remain suboptimal [BRAIN-D].

by_intent.patient_group: Patients with newly diagnosed low-grade circumscribed gliomas (e.g., pilocytic astrocytoma, ganglioglioma) following gross total resection.
by_intent.approach: Observation with serial imaging is standard after complete resection. No adjuvant therapy is indicated [GLIO-1].

by_intent.patient_group: Patients with newly diagnosed diffuse gliomas (WHO grade 2-4) after maximal safe resection.
by_intent.approach: Risk-stratified adjuvant therapy, combining radiation therapy (RT) with and/or without chemotherapy, is recommended based on histology, molecular markers (IDH, 1p/19q, MGMT), patient age, and performance status [GLIO-2 to GLIO-6, GLIO-10].

by_intent.patient_group: Patients with poor performance status (e.g., KPS <60), recurrent/refractory disease with limited options, or those who decline active treatment.
by_intent.approach: Focus on symptom management, including corticosteroids for edema, antiepileptics for seizures, pain control, and psychosocial support. Palliative radiation or single-agent systemic therapy may be considered [BRAIN-D, GLIO-11, GLIO-13].

management_principles.mdt_requirements: The NCCN panel strongly recommends multidisciplinary discussion for all patients with newly diagnosed or recurrent/progressive gliomas (any grade). Similarly, if treatment with an IDH inhibitor is being considered for a patient with newly diagnosed oligodendroglioma or astrocytoma, multidisciplinary discussion or referral to a brain tumor center is recommended [GLIO-2A footnote e]. For rare tumors like ependymoma or medulloblastoma, management should begin with consultation at a center of neuro-oncologic expertise [EPEN-1, AMED-1].
management_principles.performance_status_guide: Performance status (PS), often assessed by Karnofsky Performance Status (KPS) or ECOG score, critically influences treatment decisions. Patients with good PS (KPS ≥60 or ECOG 0-1) are generally candidates for more aggressive, multimodal therapy. Patients with poor PS (KPS <60) may receive hypofractionated RT, single-modality systemic therapy, or best supportive care. For example, in newly diagnosed glioblastoma, standard concurrent RT/temozolomide is recommended for KPS ≥70, while hypofractionated RT ± temozolomide or temozolomide alone (if MGMT methylated) is preferred for patients >70 years or those with poor PS [GLIO-10, GLIO-11].

nodes.criteria: Maximal safe resection feasible

adjuvant_logic.summary: If gross total resection, observation. If incomplete resection or biopsy, consider observation or radiation therapy for symptomatic/progressive disease. Consider BRAF/MEK inhibitors for BRAF-altered tumors [GLIO-1].

management_pathways.title: Newly Diagnosed Oligodendroglioma, WHO Grade 2 (IDH-mutant, 1p19q codeleted)

nodes.criteria: Good PS (KPS ≥60), no residual/measurable disease after surgery

adjuvant_logic.summary: Observation is reasonable. IDH inhibitor may be considered to delay need for RT/chemotherapy [GLIO-2, GLIO-2A footnote m,n].

nodes.criteria: Good PS (KPS ≥60), residual/measurable disease or upfront RT/chemo preferred

adjuvant_logic.summary: RT + adjuvant PCV is preferred based on RTOG 9802 results [GLIO-2A].

nodes.criteria: Poor PS (KPS <60)

adjuvant_logic.summary: Treatment choice depends on cause of poor PS. If due to tumor, RT+TMZ may be preferred; if due to comorbidities, IDH inhibitor may be better tolerated [GLIO-2A footnote p].

management_pathways.title: Newly Diagnosed Glioblastoma, IDH-wild-type, WHO Grade 4

nodes.criteria: Age ≤70, good PS (KPS ≥70), MGMT methylated or unmethylated

options.label: RT + concurrent temozolomide and adjuvant temozolomide + TTF (preferred)

adjuvant_logic.summary: Stupp protocol with TTF preferred. Clinical trial preferred [GLIO-10].

nodes.criteria: Age >70, good PS (KPS ≥70), MGMT methylated

options.label: Hypofractionated RT + concurrent and adjuvant temozolomide
Standard RT + concurrent and adjuvant temozolomide + TTF

adjuvant_logic.summary: Hypofractionated RT/TEM is standard. Consider TTF if good PS [GLIO-11].

nodes.criteria: Age >70, good PS (KPS ≥70), MGMT unmethylated or indeterminate

options.label: Hypofractionated RT + concurrent and adjuvant temozolomide
Standard RT + concurrent and adjuvant temozolomide + TTF

adjuvant_logic.summary: Hypofractionated RT/TEM is standard. Consider TTF if good PS [GLIO-11].

nodes.criteria: Poor PS (KPS <60) regardless of age

options.label: Hypofractionated RT ± concurrent or adjuvant temozolomide

adjuvant_logic.summary: Treatment individualized; RT alone or TMZ alone may be used. Supportive care is an option [GLIO-11].

nodes.criteria: Gross total resection, MRI spine negative, CSF negative

options.label: Standard RT (if PFA or high-grade) or observe

adjuvant_logic.summary: RT recommended for PFA or high-grade. Observation may be considered for non-PFA low-grade with gross total resection [EPEN-2].

nodes.criteria: Subtotal resection or biopsy, or evidence of metastasis

options.label: Consider reoperation, then standard RT or craniospinal RT

adjuvant_logic.summary: RT is standard adjuvant therapy. Craniospinal RT for metastatic disease [EPEN-2].

nodes.criteria: Standard risk: No metastasis, small residual (<1.5 cm²), classic/desmoplastic histology

options.label: Standard-dose craniospinal RT with boost + systemic therapy
Reduced-dose craniospinal RT with systemic therapy followed by post-RT systemic therapy

adjuvant_logic.summary: Systemic therapy with cisplatin-based regimen recommended after RT [AMED-2].

nodes.criteria: High risk: Unresectable/residual >1.5 cm², disseminated disease, or large cell histology

options.label: Craniospinal RT with systemic therapy followed by post-RT systemic therapy

adjuvant_logic.summary: Aggressive multimodal therapy [AMED-2].

nodes.criteria: Suitable for high-dose methotrexate, no ocular disease

options.label: Whole-brain RT if not candidate for chemotherapy

adjuvant_logic.summary: Induction with HD-methotrexate, then consolidation based on response [PCNS-2A].

nodes.criteria: Primary vitreoretinal lymphoma without other CNS involvement

options.label: Consider intraocular therapy, ocular RT, or systemic therapy

adjuvant_logic.summary: Multidisciplinary approach with ophthalmology [PCNS-2].

management_pathways.title: Newly Diagnosed Meningioma, WHO Grade 2 or 3

nodes.criteria: Grade 2, complete resection

adjuvant_logic.summary: RT considered, especially for higher-risk features [MENI-1].

nodes.criteria: Grade 2, incomplete resection; or Grade 3 regardless of resection

adjuvant_logic.summary: RT recommended [MENI-1].

nodes.criteria: Newly diagnosed or stable systemic disease, reasonable systemic options

options.label: Surgical resection for symptomatic lesions followed by SRS
CNS-active systemic therapy in select patients

adjuvant_logic.summary: SRS preferred to preserve cognition. WBRT not routinely added to SRS [LTD-2].

nodes.criteria: Good risk (KPS ≥60, minimal systemic disease, reasonable options)

adjuvant_logic.summary: Aggressive treatment may improve survival [LEPT-2].

nodes.criteria: Poor risk (KPS <60, extensive disease)

adjuvant_logic.summary: Focus on quality of life [LEPT-2].

nodes.criteria: Unilateral radiographic progression, preserved hearing in contralateral ear

options.label: RT (risk of hearing loss and secondary malignancy)

adjuvant_logic.summary: Preservation of hearing is paramount. Consider systemic therapy first [NF2-VS-1].

items.text: MRI brain with and without contrast (standard for all CNS tumors)
items.recommendation: Essential for diagnosis, treatment planning, and surveillance [BRAIN-A].

items.text: MRI spine (with contrast) if clinically indicated (e.g., ependymoma, medulloblastoma, metastatic disease)
items.recommendation: Staging for tumors with risk of leptomeningeal spread [BRAIN-A, EPEN-2, AMED-2].

items.text: Amino acid PET/CT or FDG-PET in select cases (e.g., equivocal MRI, differentiating recurrence vs. treatment effect)
items.recommendation: Useful for treatment planning and response assessment [BRAIN-A].

items.text: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis or whole-body PET/CT for metastatic workup
items.recommendation: For brain metastases of unknown primary or staging [LTD-1, MU-1].

items.text: Tissue diagnosis via maximal safe resection or stereotactic biopsy
items.recommendation: Required for all primary brain tumors. Molecular testing should include IDH, 1p/19q, MGMT, ATRX, TERT, BRAF as appropriate [BRAIN-E].

items.text: CSF analysis (cytology, flow cytometry, cell count, and molecular markers like MYD88)
items.recommendation: For suspected leptomeningeal disease or PCNSL. Lumbar puncture may be contraindicated with mass effect [PCNS-1, LEPT-1].

items.text: Ophthalmologic exam (slit-lamp) for PCNSL or suspected ocular involvement

items.recommendation: Pure tone thresholds and word recognition scores for hearing assessment [BRAIN-G].

items.text: Neurocognitive assessment (e.g., MMSE, MoCA, neuropsychological testing)
items.recommendation: Baseline and follow-up to monitor treatment effects [BRAIN-D].

items.text: Endocrine evaluation (if midline tumor or prior RT)
Referral to specialized brain tumor center for rare tumors (ependymoma, medulloblastoma)
Genetic counseling for hereditary syndromes (NF2, VHL, tuberous sclerosis)

surgery.role: Surgery is both diagnostic and therapeutic. The primary goals are: 1) to obtain sufficient tissue for accurate histopathologic and molecular diagnosis, 2) to achieve maximal safe resection to improve survival and local control, 3) to relieve mass effect and symptoms (e.g., seizures, increased intracranial pressure), and 4) to reduce tumor burden to enhance the efficacy of adjuvant therapies [BRAIN-B].
principles: Maximal safe resection is recommended whenever feasible, guided by preoperative MRI and intraoperative adjuncts (neuronavigation, intraoperative MRI, awake mapping, fluorescence-guided surgery) [BRAIN-B].
For diffuse gliomas, supramarginal resection (including T2/FLAIR abnormalities) is suggested for IDH-mutant tumors [BRAIN-B footnote 1].
For ependymoma, gross total resection is a strong prognostic factor; re-resection should be considered if initial resection is incomplete [EPEN-1].
For PCNSL, stereotactic biopsy is preferred over resection, as maximal resection does not improve survival [PCNS-1].
Postoperative MRI within 48 hours (brain) and 2-3 weeks (spine) is required to assess extent of resection [BRAIN-B].
Tissue should be reviewed by an experienced neuropathologist with molecular testing [BRAIN-E].

procedures.name: Gross total resection (GTR)
procedures.indication: Primary goal for most primary brain tumors (gliomas, ependymomas, metastatic lesions) when safe [BRAIN-B].
procedures.key_complications: Infection, hemorrhage, new neurological deficits (e.g., motor, speech), seizures [BRAIN-B].

procedures.name: Stereotactic biopsy
procedures.indication: Deep-seated or inoperable lesions, or when diagnosis is uncertain [BRAIN-B].

procedures.name: Laser interstitial thermal therapy (LITT)
procedures.indication: Poor surgical candidates with relapsed brain metastases, radiation necrosis, or recurrent gliomas (category 2B) [BRAIN-B].

procedures.name: Carmustine wafer implantation
procedures.approach: Placement in resection cavity during surgery
procedures.indication: High-grade glioma resection (category 2B). Note: May impact clinical trial eligibility [GLIO-9].
procedures.key_complications: Increased risk of seizures and infection [GLIO-9].

surgery.resectability_criteria: Resectability depends on tumor location, proximity to eloquent cortex, patient performance status, and expected benefit. Factors include: 1) Age and PS, 2) Tumor pathology (benign vs. malignant), 3) Feasibility of decreasing mass effect, 4) Number and location of lesions, 5) Time since last surgery for recurrent disease [BRAIN-B]. For NF2-SWN, resectability of vestibular schwannoma depends on size, hearing status, and risk of bilateral hearing loss [BRAIN-G].
surgery.pre_operative_optimisation: Optimize performance status with corticosteroids for edema. Antiepileptic drugs may be used perioperatively if seizure history. Prophylactic antibiotics should be considered. Consult neuroanesthesia and neurocritical care as needed. For spinal metastases, preoperative embolization may be considered for vascular tumors (e.g., renal cell carcinoma) [BRAIN-B, SPINE-2].

radiation_therapy.role: RT is a cornerstone of treatment for many primary and metastatic CNS tumors. It is used adjuvantly after surgery, as definitive therapy, and in the recurrent setting. The goal is to maximize tumor control while minimizing toxicity to surrounding normal brain or spinal cord [BRAIN-C].
principles: Treatment planning should be based on pre- and postoperative MRI, with the target volume defined by the gross tumor volume (GTV) and appropriate clinical target volume (CTV) margins [BRAIN-C].
Conformal techniques (3D-CRT, IMRT, VMAT, proton therapy) are recommended to spare critical structures [BRAIN-C].
For diffuse gliomas, tumor volumes are defined using T2/FLAIR and contrast-enhanced T1 sequences [BRAIN-C].
For brain metastases, SRS is preferred for limited disease to preserve cognition. WBRT is reserved for diffuse disease or poor prognosis [BRAIN-C].
Hypofractionated RT is appropriate for elderly or frail patients [BRAIN-C].
Reirradiation is feasible in select cases, with careful attention to cumulative doses and interval from prior RT [BRAIN-C].

dose_frameworks.name: Standard fractionated RT for gliomas
dose_frameworks.indication: Adjuvant treatment for WHO grade 2-4 gliomas [BRAIN-C].

dose_frameworks.name: Hypofractionated RT for elderly/frail
dose_frameworks.indication: Glioblastoma in elderly or poor PS patients [BRAIN-C].

dose_frameworks.name: WBRT for brain metastases
dose_frameworks.indication: Diffuse brain metastases or poor prognosis [BRAIN-C].

dose_frameworks.name: Craniospinal RT for medulloblastoma/ependymoma
dose_frameworks.indication: Medulloblastoma or ependymoma with metastatic disease [BRAIN-C].

approaches.name: Adjuvant RT for glioblastoma
approaches.dose_fractionation: 60 Gy in 30 fractions or 59.4 Gy in 33 fractions
approaches.toxicities: Fatigue, alopecia, cognitive effects, edema, nausea [BRAIN-C].

approaches.name: SRS for brain metastases
approaches.dose_fractionation: 18-24 Gy single fraction or 24-30 Gy in 3-5 fractions

approaches.name: HA-WBRT + memantine
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: Memantine 20 mg daily during and 6 months after RT
approaches.indication: Brain metastases not within 5 mm of hippocampi, KPS ≥70, life expectancy ≥4 months [BRAIN-C].

approaches.name: Reirradiation for recurrent glioblastoma
approaches.dose_fractionation: 35 Gy in 10 fractions (fractionated SRT) or SRS (15-18 Gy)
approaches.indication: Recurrent glioblastoma, especially if new lesion outside prior field or geometrically favorable [BRAIN-C].

systemic_therapy.overview: Systemic therapy for CNS tumors includes alkylating agents, targeted therapies, immunotherapies, and intrathecal treatments. Blood-brain barrier penetration is a key consideration. Treatment is often based on histology and molecular markers [BRAIN-D, GLIO-A].

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Adjuvant treatment, WHO grade 2 oligodendroglioma (IDH-mutant, 1p19q codeleted), KPS ≥60
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Significant OS benefit shown in RTOG 9802 [120].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: RT + adjuvant temozolomide; RT + concurrent and adjuvant temozolomide

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Adjuvant treatment, glioblastoma, IDH-wild-type, KPS ≥60, age ≤70
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: RT + concurrent temozolomide and adjuvant temozolomide + TTF (category 1)
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Improved OS and PFS in EF-14 trial [178, 179].

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Bevacizumab; Temozolomide (if prior long interval); Lomustine; Carmustine; PCV; IDH inhibitor (if IDH-mutant)
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Multiple options with varying evidence. Bevacizumab improves PFS [188-190].
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: MGMT methylation may predict response to alkylating agents

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Relapsed or refractory primary CNS lymphoma
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: High-dose methotrexate rechallenge (if prior long response); Other systemic therapies (ibrutinib, rituximab, temozolomide, lenalidomide, pomalidomide); High-dose chemotherapy with stem cell rescue (category 2B)
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: No standard of care; clinical trial preferred [PCNS-A].

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Brain metastases from NSCLC with EGFR mutation
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Excellent CNS activity in clinical trials [541-547, 48].

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Brain metastases from melanoma with BRAF V600E mutation

by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Bevacizumab shows hearing improvement and tumor reduction [1, 2].

treatment_response_assessment.title: Response Assessment in Neuro-Oncology (RANO)
treatment_response_assessment.timing: Post-treatment MRI brain within 24-48 hours for surgical extent assessment. Follow-up MRI at 2-8 weeks after RT completion, then every 2-4 months for 3 years, then every 3-6 months indefinitely for high-grade gliomas [BRAIN-A]. For low-grade gliomas, intervals may be longer (every 3-6 months initially) [BRAIN-A].
response_logic: Pseudoprogression (treatment-related increase in enhancement/edema) can mimic tumor progression, especially in the first 3-6 months after RT or with concurrent temozolomide. It is more common in MGMT-methylated glioblastoma [200, 201].
If progression is suspected, consider advanced imaging (MR perfusion, spectroscopy, PET) or biopsy to confirm true progression vs. treatment effect [BRAIN-D].
For leptomeningeal metastases, response is assessed by CSF cytology (conversion to negative) and clinical/neuroimaging improvement [LEPT-3].
For brain metastases, response is assessed by RECIST criteria on MRI [BRAIN-A].
imaging_recommendations: MRI brain with and without contrast is the standard for follow-up [BRAIN-A].
For ependymoma: MRI brain/spine every 3-4 months for 1 year, then every 4-6 months for year 2, then every 6-12 months for 5-10 years [EPEN-4].
For medulloblastoma: Brain MRI every 2-3 months for 2 years; then every 6-12 months for 5-10 years [AMED-3].

For meningiomas: Postoperative MRI within 48 hours; then WHO grade 1/2: MRI at 1, 3, 6 months, then every 6-12 months for 5 years; WHO grade 3: MRI every 2-4 months for 3 years, then every 3-6 months [BRAIN-A].
biopsy_or_salvage_logic: Biopsy should be considered when imaging suggests progression but treatment effect is possible, to confirm recurrence and assess for transformation (e.g., low-grade to high-grade glioma) [GLIO-7, GLIO-13].
For recurrent brain metastases, if recurrence is suspected after SRS, advanced imaging or biopsy is recommended before considering further radiation [BRAIN-C].
Salvage options depend on prior therapy and may include re-resection, reirradiation, systemic therapy, or clinical trial [GLIO-13, LTD-3, MU-2].

prognosis.overall: Prognosis for CNS cancers is highly variable depending on tumor type, histologic grade, molecular features, patient age, and performance status. Glioblastoma, the most common malignant primary brain tumor in adults, has a 5-year survival rate around 6%, with higher rates among younger age groups. WHO grade 2-3 gliomas generally have better outcomes, particularly those with favorable molecular profiles. Brain metastases prognosis depends on primary tumor type, number and volume of metastases, patient performance status, and extracranial disease control. Primary CNS lymphoma prognosis has improved with treatment advances. Meningiomas, most of which are WHO grade 1, generally have favorable outcomes with surgical resection. Ependymomas and medulloblastomas in adults are rare but have distinct prognostic profiles based on molecular subtyping.

by_stage.five_yr_survival: Significantly improved compared to other gliomas
by_stage.context: Favorable prognosis conferred by both IDH mutation and 1p/19q codeletion; patients may remain progression-free for many years after gross total resection

by_stage.five_yr_survival: Significantly better than other high-grade gliomas
by_stage.context: In EORTC 26951, OS not reached in the RT+PCV group vs 112 months in the RT group for 1p/19q codeleted subgroup
Prognosis is intermediate between oligodendroglioma and glioblastoma; IDH mutation confers favorable prognosis
Despite grade 4 histologic features, these tumors still have better prognosis than IDH wild-type glioblastomas
Median OS 14.6 months with RT+TMZ (EORTC-NCIC); 5-year survival 10% with RT+TMZ vs 2% with RT alone
Hypofractionated RT+TMZ: median OS 9.3 months vs 7.6 months RT alone; MGMT-methylated tumors benefit most
Complete response to chemotherapy ranges 42%-61%; OS ranges 14-55 months
10-year survival rates over 50% after RT; local recurrence is primary pattern of failure
10-year PFS 75% after GTR; short-term recurrence rates 1%-16%
10-year PFS drops to 39% with STR; short-term recurrence rates 20%-41%
SRS preferred for limited disease; local and distant brain control are primary concerns

prognostic_factors: IDH1/2 mutation status: favorable prognostic marker for OS in grade 2-3 gliomas
1p/19q codeletion: confers favorable prognosis and predictive of response to alkylating systemic therapy with or without RT
MGMT promoter methylation: strong prognostic biomarker and predictive for benefit from temozolomide in high-grade gliomas
ATRX mutation: associated with IDH mutations; loss of ATRX immunostaining suggests astrocytoma
TERT promoter mutation: associated with reduced OS in IDH wild-type gliomas
H3K27M mutation: associated with very poor prognosis regardless of histologic appearance
Extent of resection: GTR associated with decreased mortality and lower risk of progression in low-grade gliomas
Karnofsky Performance Status: KPS ≥60 vs <60 determines treatment intensity
Age: older age associated with worse prognosis in glioblastoma
CDKN2A/B homozygous deletion: associated with grade 4 status in IDH-mutant astrocytomas
Medulloblastoma molecular subtype: WNT-activated associated with markedly better prognosis
Meningioma grade and extent of resection: Simpson classification correlates with recurrence
Brain metastasis volume and rate of developing new metastases: more important than number for prognosis
prognosis.prognostic_context: Molecular profiling has fundamentally transformed prognostication in CNS cancers. The 2021 WHO classification integrates molecular markers that provide greater prognostic separation than histology alone. For diffuse gliomas, the hierarchy of prognosis is: IDH-mutant, 1p19q codeleted (oligodendroglioma) has the best prognosis, followed by IDH-mutant without codeletion (astrocytoma), with IDH wild-type (glioblastoma) having the worst prognosis. MGMT promoter methylation is both prognostic and predictive for benefit from alkylating chemotherapy. In brain metastases, total volume of disease and rate of developing new metastases may be more important prognostic factors than number of discrete metastases.

disease_related.management: Corticosteroid therapy with careful monitoring; lowest dose for shortest time; patients with extensive mass effect should receive steroids for at least 24 hours before RT; consider H2 blockers or PPI for high GI risk patients

Seizure prophylaxis NOT recommended as routine in asymptomatic patients; reasonable to consider perioperatively; avoid enzyme-inducing antiepileptic drugs (phenytoin, phenobarbital, carbamazepine); prefer non-EIAEDs (levetiracetam, topiramate, valproic acid, lacosamide)

Occurs in first 3 months or longer after completion of RT; more often in MGMT promoter methylated tumors; biopsy, MR spectroscopy, MR perfusion, or brain PET/CT can differentiate from true progression
Tends to be detected between 6 and 24 months following RT treatment; consider bevacizumab if symptoms do not resolve with corticosteroids; consider hyperbaric oxygen; consider LITT
VP shunt placement if needed; neurosurgical evaluation
Medical emergency; steroids (minimum 4 mg dexamethasone every 6 hours); decompressive surgery followed by RT is preferred (category 1); primary RT for radiosensitive tumors

Common in brain tumor patients; evaluate hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis, thyroid, and gonad function; long-term monitoring of hypothalamic-pituitary and adrenal axis may be considered for patients who received prior RT

Up to 90% of individuals with supratentorial brain tumors experience some degree; neuropsychological evaluation is gold standard; consider neuropsychological evaluation as needed based on physician assessment
Depends on location and cause; surgical evaluation for structural lesions
Multidisciplinary care including otologists; bevacizumab results in hearing improvement in 36% of NF2-SWN patients; CI or ABI may be considered; auditory rehabilitation
Prognosis-dependent treatment; CSF analysis; systemic therapy, intra-CSF therapy, and/or RT as appropriate

chemotherapy.management: Monitor CBC; dose modifications as needed; PCV regimen associated with significant myelosuppression; 70% of patients in EORTC 26951 did not complete planned 6 cycles of PCV
Consider glucarpidase (carboxypeptidase G2) for prolonged methotrexate clearance due to methotrexate-induced renal toxicity
Screen for underlying medical causes; physical exercise has best evidence for prevention and treatment; consider psychostimulants for attention improvement
Monitor liver function; hold if significant elevation
Oral care protocols; dose modifications as needed

immunotherapy.management: Monitor for organ-specific toxicities; corticosteroids as needed; ipilimumab/nivolumab combination for melanoma brain metastases associated with grade 3/4 treatment-related adverse events in just over half of patients

targeted_therapy.management: Monitor for pulmonary adverse events; 180 mg once daily with 7-day lead-in at 90 mg for brigatinib to reduce early-onset moderate to severe pulmonary adverse events

surgery.management: Careful surgical planning; use of surgical adjuncts (intraoperative microscope, stereotactic image guidance, fMRI, awake craniotomy, motor/speech mapping)
Standard perioperative antibiotics; monitor for signs of infection
Watertight dural closure when carmustine wafers used; sufficient steroids and antiepileptics in perioperative period

radiation.management: Use cognitive-sparing techniques (HA-WBRT with memantine when feasible); SRS preferred over WBRT for limited metastases to avoid cognitive decline; memantine during WBRT for patients with favorable prognosis
Detected between 6 and 24 months; consider bevacizumab, hyperbaric oxygen, or LITT
Long-term monitoring of hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis for patients who received prior RT
Increased lifetime risk of CNS malignancy (~6%) in irradiated patients with NF2-SWN; minimize radiation fields
Minimize dose to cochlea; consider proton therapy when available

supportive_care.overview: Supportive care is integral to comprehensive management of CNS cancers. The NCCN panel emphasizes that throughout treatment, the patient's quality of life should remain the highest priority and guide clinical decision-making. The multidisciplinary team approach is essential, incorporating neuro-oncology, surgery, radiation, radiology, neuropsychology, rehabilitation medicine, palliative care, and psychosocial support services. Management of complications such as seizures, edema, neurocognitive dysfunction, endocrinopathies, venous thromboembolism, fatigue, and psychiatric disease requires systematic attention.
supportive_care.nutritional_support: Nutritional assessment and support should be integrated into comprehensive care. Patients with brain tumors may experience difficulty with oral intake due to neurologic deficits, treatment side effects, or medication effects. Nutritional supplementation and dietary counseling should be provided as needed. Multidisciplinary nutritional support teams should be involved for patients with significant nutritional compromise.
supportive_care.anti_emetic_protocol: Standard antiemetic protocols should be used based on the emetogenic potential of specific systemic therapy regimens. Corticosteroids used for cerebral edema may have some antiemetic properties. Specific antiemetic guidelines for chemotherapy-induced nausea and vomiting should be followed per institutional protocols.
supportive_care.gcsf_guidance: Granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (GCSF) should be considered for patients experiencing significant myelosuppression, particularly those receiving PCV chemotherapy for low-grade gliomas. In EORTC 26951, 70% of patients in the RT followed by PCV arm did not complete the planned six cycles of treatment due to toxicity. Dose modifications and growth factor support should be individualized based on patient tolerance.
supportive_care.vte_prophylaxis: Venous thromboembolism is common in brain tumor patients. Treatment with direct oral anticoagulants is appropriate for brain cancer patients unless there is active bleeding or history of recent bleeding (recommend consultation with neurosurgeon). Referral to the NCCN Guidelines for Cancer-Associated Venous Thromboembolic Disease for detailed management.
supportive_care.pain_management: Pain management should be integrated into comprehensive care from diagnosis through survivorship. Pain may be due to tumor mass effect, treatment-related effects, or neuropathic causes. For metastatic spine tumors, three types of pain are classically defined: local pain (deep aching, improves with steroids), mechanical back pain (varies with movement/position, attributed to structural instability), and radicular pain (sharp/stabbing from nerve root compression). Multimodal pain management including pharmacologic and interventional approaches should be utilized.
supportive_care.psychosocial_support: Depression and anxiety are common in neuro-oncology patients, greater than simple sadness or anxiety associated with tumor diagnosis. These symptoms respond to psychotropic medications. Physical exercise can help reduce anxiety and improve mood. All oncology providers should inquire about psychiatric symptoms in follow-up visits. Refer to the NCCN Guidelines for Distress Management including NCCN Distress Thermometer. Communication between patient's healthcare team members regarding treatment response is important.
supportive_care.dental_care: Oral care is important for patients receiving systemic therapy, particularly those on temozolomide who may develop stomatitis. Dental evaluation and maintenance should be recommended prior to initiation of systemic therapy. Oral care protocols should be implemented during treatment.

follow_up.post_curative_treatment: After curative-intent treatment for primary brain tumors, patients should be followed with serial brain MRI scans and clinical assessment. For glioblastoma: brain MRI 2-8 weeks after standard RT, then every 2-4 months for 3 years, then every 3-6 months indefinitely. For IDH-mutant astrocytoma WHO grade 4: same schedule. For lower-grade gliomas: monitoring frequency depends on molecular subtype and treatment received. Early scans (first 3 months) may show pseudoprogression and should not automatically trigger change in therapy. After brain metastases treatment: brain MRI every 2-3 months for 1-2 years, then every 4-6 months indefinitely.
follow_up.surveillance_rationale: Surveillance imaging serves multiple purposes: 1) Early detection of tumor recurrence to enable timely intervention; 2) Monitoring for treatment effects including pseudoprogression (common in first 3 months post-RT, particularly in MGMT-methylated tumors) and radiation necrosis (typically 6-24 months post-RT); 3) Assessment of response to systemic therapy; 4) Titration of corticosteroid doses based on extent of mass effect and brain edema. The treating provider should have flexibility to determine appropriate imaging frequency based on individual patient circumstances.
late_effects_screening: Neurocognitive assessment: neuropsychological evaluation as needed; cognitive deterioration is a sensitive indicator of tumor progression
Endocrine monitoring: long-term monitoring of hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis (ACTH stimulation test), thyroid function, gonad function
Seizure management: long-term anti-epileptic medication management; periodic assessment of seizure control
Functional status: ongoing assessment of performance status, ADLs, and quality of life
Radiation necrosis monitoring: serial MRIs to distinguish from tumor progression
Secondary malignancy screening: particularly relevant for NF2-SWN patients who received radiation (~6% lifetime risk)
Dental health: monitoring for oral complications of treatment
Psychosocial screening: depression, anxiety, cognitive changes
Bone health: monitoring for osteoporosis in patients on chronic steroids
follow_up.recurrence_patterns: Recurrence patterns vary by tumor type: Glioblastoma typically recurs locally within the prior radiation field; however, 20-30% may recur at distant sites. Low-grade gliomas may transform to higher-grade gliomas over time, with approximately half undergoing anaplastic transformation within 5 years. Brain metastases may recur locally at treated sites or develop as new distant lesions. Primary CNS lymphoma recurrence occurs in about half of patients who initially achieved complete response. Meningioma recurrence depends on grade and extent of resection (Simpson classification).

clinical_follow_up_schedule: Glioblastoma: Brain MRI every 2-3 months for 3 years, then every 2-4 months indefinitely
IDH-mutant astrocytoma grade 4: Brain MRI 2-8 weeks after standard RT, then every 2-4 months for 3 years, then every 3-6 months indefinitely
Lower-grade gliomas (grade 2 oligodendroglioma/astrocytoma): After RT and chemotherapy, at least every 6-9 months until progression; after surgery only, every 3-4 months until progression
Oligodendroglioma grade 3: After RT and chemotherapy, at least every 6-9 months until progression
Ependymoma: Imaging of tumor site every 3-4 months for 1 year, then every 4-6 months for year 2, then every 6-12 months for 5-10 years, then as clinically indicated
Medulloblastoma: Brain MRI every 2-3 months for 2 years, then every 6-12 months for 5-10 years, then every 1-2 years or as clinically indicated
Primary CNS lymphoma: Brain MRI every 3 months until year 2, then every 6 months until 5 years, then annually indefinitely
Meningiomas grade 1-2: Brain MRI at 1, 3, and 6 months, then every 6-12 months for 5 years, then every 1-3 years as clinically indicated
Meningiomas grade 3: Brain MRI every 2-4 months for 3 years, then every 3-6 months
Limited brain metastases: Brain MRI every 2-3 months for 1-2 years, then every 4-6 months indefinitely
Extensive brain metastases: Brain MRI every 2-3 months for 1-2 years, then every 4-6 months indefinitely
Leptomeningeal metastases: Neuroimaging every 2-3 months until year 2, every 6 months until 5 years, then annually indefinitely
Metastatic spine tumors: Spine MRI/CT 1-3 months after treatment, then every 3-4 months for 1 year, then as clinically indicated
imaging_strategy: MRI is the standard imaging modality for all CNS tumor surveillance
Brain MRI with and without IV gadolinium-based contrast is recommended for most CNS tumors
Spine MRI when clinically indicated or for staging purposes
Postoperative brain MRI within 48 hours for gliomas and other brain tumors to determine extent of resection
Spine MRI should be delayed by at least 2-3 weeks post surgery to avoid artifacts
Consider PET-MRI every 3-6 months for meningiomas
Amino acid PET/CT or PET/MRI may be used for equivocal cases to differentiate treatment effects from tumor recurrence
MR perfusion, MR spectroscopy, and FDG-PET may help differentiate pseudoprogression/radiation necrosis from true progression
Brain MRI should be obtained as early as possible if patient experiences change in seizures, new/worsening neurologic signs, or new/higher dose steroid requirements
laboratory_monitoring: CBC and metabolic panel: monitor regularly during systemic therapy, particularly with temozolomide and PCV regimens
CSF analysis: indicated when clinical concern for meningeal dissemination
For primary CNS lymphoma: CBC, comprehensive metabolic panel, LDH test, HIV status
For leptomeningeal metastases: CSF cytology via lumbar puncture; sensitivity approximately 50% with first LP, up to 90% with repeated analyses
CSF flow scan if concerns about CSF flow blockage before intrathecal therapy
Hepatitis B testing before rituximab therapy due to risk of reactivation
supportive_follow_up: Regular neurocognitive assessment; neuropsychological evaluation when indicated
Endocrine function monitoring, especially for patients who received cranial RT
Seizure control assessment and medication management
Psychiatric symptom screening for depression and anxiety
Ophthalmologic exams for NF2-SWN and PCNSL patients with ocular involvement
Vocal fold evaluation prior to VS surgery for NF2-SWN patients

special_situations.situation: Glioblastoma in older adults (>70 years) with MGMT-methylated tumor
special_situations.key_message: Hypofractionated RT plus concurrent and adjuvant temozolomide is preferred for elderly patients with good PS and MGMT-methylated tumors
special_situations.management: Hypofractionated RT (34 Gy in 10 fractions) plus concurrent and adjuvant TMZ; or standard RT plus concurrent and adjuvant TMZ and alternating electric field therapy (category 1); TMZ alone as initial therapy may be reasonable for those who prefer to delay radiation
special_situations.evidence: Nordic trial: patients >70 years with MGMT-methylated tumors had better survival with TMZ compared to standard RT (median OS 9.7 vs 6.8 months; HR 0.56; P=.02). Perry et al phase III trial: hypofractionated RT+TMZ showed PFS 5.3 vs 3.9 months (HR 0.50) and median OS 9.3 vs 7.6 months (HR 0.67)

special_situations.situation: HIV-positive patients with primary CNS lymphoma
special_situations.key_message: HIV-positive patients with PCNSL require concurrent antiretroviral therapy with cancer treatment
special_situations.management: Antiretroviral (ARV) therapy should be part of treatment; ARVs can be administered safely with systemic therapy; consultation with HIV specialist or pharmacist for optimization
special_situations.evidence: See NCCN Guidelines for Cancer in People with HIV for specific management guidance

special_situations.key_message: CNS-active systemic therapies may be considered upfront in select asymptomatic patients with EGFR-mutated NSCLC brain metastases
special_situations.management: Osimertinib preferred for EGFR-sensitizing mutation positive disease; in FLAURA trial, CNS ORR 91% with osimertinib vs 68% with standard EGFR-TKI; consider deferral of radiation with close MRI surveillance and multidisciplinary discussion
special_situations.evidence: FLAURA trial: median PFS with osimertinib 15.2 vs 9.6 months standard EGFR-TKI (HR 0.47) in patients with brain metastases; AURA3: median PFS 8.5 vs 4.2 months (HR 0.32) for osimertinib vs platinum-pemetrexed in T790M-positive patients

special_situations.situation: Brain metastases from HER2-positive breast cancer
special_situations.key_message: Tucatinib-based regimen is preferred for previously treated HER2-positive breast cancer with brain metastases
special_situations.management: Capecitabine/tucatinib + trastuzumab (category 1 if previously treated with ≥1 regimen); fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki if previously treated
special_situations.evidence: HER2CLIMB trial: tucatinib arm showed superior PFS (HR 0.46) and OS (HR 0.58) in patients with brain metastases; 1-year PFS 24.9% vs 0% with placebo

special_situations.situation: Brain metastases from BRAF V600-mutant melanoma
special_situations.key_message: BRAF/MEK inhibitor combination therapy has demonstrated intracranial activity for BRAF-mutant melanoma brain metastases
special_situations.management: Dabrafenib/trametinib preferred; cobimetinib/vemurafenib (category 2B); ipilimumab + nivolumab for BRAF non-specific disease
special_situations.evidence: COMBI-MB trial: dabrafenib+trametinib showed intracranial response in 58% of untreated asymptomatic and 56% of previously treated asymptomatic brain metastases

special_situations.key_message: Second- and third-generation ALK inhibitors preferred due to better CNS activity profiles
special_situations.management: Brigatinib, lorlatinib, alectinib, ceritinib; lorlatinib showed 61% complete CNS response in CROWN trial vs 15% with crizotinib
special_situations.evidence: CROWN trial: lorlatinib vs crizotinib for ALK-positive NSCLC; complete CNS response in 61% vs 15% in patients with brain metastases at baseline

special_situations.situation: Patients with NF2-related schwannomatosis and vestibular schwannomas
special_situations.key_message: Multidisciplinary management is essential; radiation should be considered carefully due to increased risk of CNS malignancy (~6%) in irradiated NF2-SWN patients
special_situations.management: Bevacizumab preferred for progressive VS (hearing improvement in 36%); surgery for resectable lesions; minimize radiation fields; consider proton therapy; audiologic monitoring every 6 months
special_situations.evidence: Plotkin et al JCO 2019: high-dose bevacizumab in NF2-SWN: partial VS volume reduction ≥20% in 40%, disease stabilization in 50%

special_situations.situation: Glioblastoma with de novo DNA replication repair deficiency
special_situations.key_message: This small subgroup (~2% of glioblastomas) may benefit from immune checkpoint blockade
special_situations.management: DNA replication repair deficiency testing recommended for newly diagnosed glioblastoma if TMB-H; test for Lynch syndrome if positive; consider immune checkpoint blockade
special_situations.evidence: Hadad et al Acta Neuropathol 2023: this distinct GBM subtype may benefit from immune checkpoint blockade

special_situations.situation: Recurrent or progressive WHO grade 3/4 glioma amenable to reirradiation
special_situations.key_message: Reirradiation can be performed safely in select circumstances; requires careful attention to cumulative doses and interval from prior RT
special_situations.management: Consider clinical trial or systemic therapy; reirradiation in select cases if new lesion outside target of prior RT or recurrence is small and geometrically favorable; highly focused techniques required; minimum 6 months between courses for glioblastoma (typical for same area)
special_situations.evidence: RTOG 1205: concurrent bevacizumab and reirradiation showed possibility of decreased risk for neurotoxicity

special_situations.situation: Meningioma with progression despite surgery and RT
special_situations.key_message: Systemic therapy options are limited; enrollment in clinical trials is encouraged
special_situations.management: Sunitinib (category 2B), bevacizumab, everolimus+bevacizumab (category 2B), abemaciclib, somatostatin analogues
special_situations.evidence: Kaley et al 2025: abemaciclib showed activity in recurrent meningioma in multicenter basket trial; 6-month PFS rate 42% with sunitinib in heavily pretreated patients

clinical_pearls: MGMT promoter methylation status is both prognostic and predictive for benefit from alkylating chemotherapy (temozolomide) in high-grade gliomas. Patients with MGMT-methylated tumors derive greater benefit from temozolomide-based regimens.

Molecular testing is now integral to diagnosis and treatment planning for gliomas. IDH1/2 mutation testing, 1p/19q codeletion, ATRX, TERT promoter, and MGMT methylation should be standard. The presence of an IDH mutation indicates at least a grade 2 diffusely infiltrative glioma.

For patients with low-grade glioma (grade 2 oligodendroglioma or astrocytoma), RTOG 9802 demonstrated that RT followed by 6 cycles of PCV significantly improved median OS (13.3 vs 7.8 years; P=.02) compared to RT alone. This is a category 1 recommendation for high-risk patients.

Maximum safe resection is recommended for brain tumors whenever possible. For low-grade gliomas, GTR is associated with decreased mortality and lower risk of disease progression. Postoperative MRI should be performed within 48 hours to document extent of resection.

SRS is generally preferred over WBRT for limited brain metastases due to superior cognitive preservation and comparable survival. Routine addition of WBRT to SRS is not recommended due to increased cognitive toxicity without OS benefit.

Hippocampal avoidance with WBRT plus memantine is the preferred WBRT approach for patients with favorable prognosis (life expectancy ≥4 months, no metastases within 5 mm of hippocampi) based on the NRG-CC001 trial demonstrating significantly lower cognitive failure risk.

For primary CNS lymphoma, high-dose methotrexate-based regimens are the standard induction therapy. Steroids should be withheld prior to biopsy if possible, as they are cytolytic and can significantly decrease tumor enhancement and affect histologic appearance.

Pseudoprogression occurs in the first 3 months or longer after completion of RT and can be distinguished from true progression using MR spectroscopy, MR perfusion, PET/CT, or tissue sampling. It occurs more often in patients whose tumors are MGMT promoter methylated.

key_trials.full_name: Radiotherapy plus concomitant and adjuvant temozolomide for glioblastoma
key_trials.population: Newly diagnosed glioblastoma, age ≤70, WHO PS ≤2
key_trials.intervention: RT (60 Gy) + concurrent daily TMZ (75 mg/m2) + adjuvant TMZ (150-200 mg/m2, 5/28 days × 6 cycles)
key_trials.key_result: Median OS 14.6 vs 12.1 months (P=.001); 2-year survival 26.5% vs 10.4%
key_trials.practice_change: Established RT+TMZ as standard of care for newly diagnosed glioblastoma

key_trials.full_name: Randomized trial of radiation therapy plus procarbazine, lomustine, and vincristine chemotherapy for supratentorial adult low-grade glioma
key_trials.journal: J Clin Oncol / N Engl J Med
key_trials.population: Supratentorial WHO grade 2 gliomas with risk factors (STR or age ≥40 years)
key_trials.key_result: Median OS 13.3 vs 7.8 years (P=.02); 10-year survival 62% vs 41%
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Significant improvement in PFS; subgroup analysis showed benefit primarily in IDH-mutant tumors
key_trials.practice_change: Category 1 recommendation for RT+PCV in high-risk low-grade glioma

key_trials.full_name: Tumor-treating fields plus maintenance temozolomide vs maintenance temozolomide alone for glioblastoma
key_trials.intervention: Standard RT+TMZ followed by TMZ + alternating electric field therapy
key_trials.comparator: Standard RT+TMZ followed by TMZ alone
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Median PFS 6.7 vs 4.0 months (HR 0.63; P<.001); no increased seizure frequency
key_trials.practice_change: Category 1 recommendation for alternating electric field therapy with TMZ for newly diagnosed glioblastoma

key_trials.full_name: Hippocampal avoidance during whole-brain radiotherapy plus memantine for patients with brain metastases
key_trials.population: Brain metastases not within 5 mm of hippocampi, KPS ≥70, life expectancy ≥4 months
key_trials.key_result: Risk of cognitive failure significantly lower with HA-WBRT (HR 0.76; 95% CI 0.60-0.98; P=.03)
key_trials.practice_change: HA-WBRT + memantine preferred over standard WBRT for eligible patients

key_trials.full_name: Tucatinib, trastuzumab, and capecitabine for HER2-positive metastatic breast cancer
key_trials.population: HER2-positive metastatic breast cancer previously treated with ≥1 HER2-directed regimen
key_trials.key_result: Median PFS 7.8 vs 5.4 months (HR 0.54); OS HR 0.73; P=.001
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: In patients with brain metastases: CNS PFS HR 0.36 (P<.00001); OS HR 0.49 (P=.004)
key_trials.practice_change: Category 1 recommendation for tucatinib-based regimen for HER2-positive breast cancer with brain metastases

key_trials.full_name: Phase 3 study of vorasidenib versus placebo in grade 2 IDH1/2-mutant glioma
key_trials.population: Residual or recurrent grade 2 IDH1/2-mutant glioma (after surgery, no prior treatment)
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Overall survival data not yet available
key_trials.practice_change: Category 1 recommendation for vorasidenib in residual/measurable disease after surgery for grade 2 IDH-mutant glioma

key_trials.full_name: Adjuvant procarbazine, lomustine, and vincristine chemotherapy in newly diagnosed anaplastic oligodendroglioma
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: In 1p/19q codeleted subgroup: OS not reached vs 112 months (HR 0.56; 95% CI 0.31-1.03)
key_trials.practice_change: Category 1 recommendation for RT + adjuvant PCV for WHO grade 3 oligodendroglioma

key_trials.full_name: Short-course radiation plus temozolomide in elderly patients with glioblastoma
key_trials.intervention: Hypofractionated RT (40 Gy/15 fractions) + concurrent and adjuvant TMZ
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: PFS 5.3 vs 3.9 months (HR 0.50; P<.001); greatest benefit in MGMT-methylated tumors
key_trials.practice_change: Hypofractionated RT+TMZ preferred for elderly glioblastoma patients

key_trials.full_name: Combined nivolumab and ipilimumab in melanoma metastatic to the brain
key_trials.journal: N Engl J Med / Lancet Oncol
key_trials.population: Melanoma with active, untreated brain metastases
key_trials.intervention: Nivolumab 1 mg/kg + ipilimumab 3 mg/kg Q3W × 4, then nivolumab 240 mg Q2W
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: 58% of responses lasted >2 years; limited response in symptomatic disease
key_trials.practice_change: Category 1 recommendation for ipilimumab + nivolumab for melanoma brain metastases

key_trials.full_name: Osimertinib in untreated EGFR-mutated advanced non-small-cell lung cancer
key_trials.key_result: Median PFS 18.9 vs 10.2 months (HR 0.46; P<.001); OS 38.6 vs 31.8 months (HR 0.80; P=.046)
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: CNS ORR 91% vs 68% (P=.066) in patients with measurable CNS lesions
key_trials.practice_change: Preferred first-line treatment for EGFR-mutated NSCLC including patients with brain metastases

key_trials.full_name: Chemoimmunotherapy with MATRix regimen in patients with primary CNS lymphoma
key_trials.key_result: CR rate 49% (95% CI 38%-60%) with MATRix vs 30% (21%-42%) and 23% (14%-31%) for other regimens
key_trials.practice_change: MATRix regimen established as preferred induction therapy for PCNSL

key_trials.full_name: Dabrafenib plus trametinib in patients with BRAF V600-mutant melanoma brain metastases
key_trials.population: Melanoma with BRAF V600-mutant brain metastases
key_trials.intervention: Dabrafenib 150 mg BID + trametinib 2 mg QD
key_trials.key_result: Intracranial ORR 58% (Cohort A: asymptomatic, untreated), 56% (Cohort B: asymptomatic, previously treated), 59% (Cohort C: symptomatic)
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Clinical benefit and acceptable toxicity across cohorts
key_trials.practice_change: Dabrafenib/trametinib is preferred for BRAF V600-mutant melanoma brain metastases

key_trials.full_name: First-line lorlatinib or crizotinib in advanced ALK-positive lung cancer
key_trials.key_result: Median PFS not reached vs 9.3 months (HR 0.19; P<.001)
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: In patients with brain metastases (n=78): complete CNS response 61% vs 15% with lorlatinib vs crizotinib
key_trials.practice_change: Lorlatinib preferred for ALK-positive NSCLC, particularly with brain metastases

key_trials.full_name: Effect of single-fraction vs multifraction radiotherapy on ambulatory status among patients with spinal canal compression
key_trials.population: Metastatic cancer with spinal cord or cauda equina compression
key_trials.key_result: Single-fraction 8 Gy did not meet criteria for noninferiority for ambulatory status
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Similar efficacy with different fractionation schedules
key_trials.practice_change: Multifraction schedules preferred for spinal cord compression in general, though single-fraction may be appropriate for selected patients
